responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La comunità terapeutica. Tra mito e realtà [The therapeutic community: between myth and reality]

Anna Ferruta, Giovanni Foresti, Enrico Pedriali and Marta Vigorelli. Milan: Raffaelo Cortina Editore. 1998. Pp. 623

Mario Monti

Mario Monti

Via G. Vico 13, 50136 Florence

Mario Monti  

The authors of this book, which contains over forty contributions and is a follow-up to a congress with the same title held in Milan in 1996, here discuss the results of the revolution represented by therapeutic communities in psychiatry. The sub-title, ‘Between myth and reality’, emphasises the need to determine the extent to which the myths of the anti-institutional psychiatry of the 1960s are reflected in the practice of community psychiatry. On the basis of the notion that the term ‘therapeutic community’ has come to be surrounded by a mythical halo, a kind of ‘caravanserai’ of meanings, the book stresses the need to return from the excess of the sense of myth to reality, establishing a minimum common denominator from the many different experiences and developing a common conceptual basis from the integration of the reference models. The volume is divided into four sections. The first, which is historical and geographical in character, examines the historical background to the development of the experience of the therapeutic community in British, French and Italian psychiatry. The second analyses the therapeutic factors operating in community treatment and distinguishes between the various types of communities (for adolescents, for young adult psychotics, and for chronic psychotics). The book ends with a section on the problem of training of those who work in this field in their various tasks and another on the meaning that may be assumed by functionality evaluation processes in a psychiatric unit.

The revolution represented by the institution of the therapeutic community is one of a long line of ‘revolutions’ that have punctuated the history of psychiatry. The word ‘revolution’, we find, is used again and again to indicate the discontinuities and radical turning points where psychiatry broke with the past and new approaches to mental suffering opened up. In this sense, every revolution seemed capable of opening the way to apparently decisive therapeutic possibilities and at the same time of conferring greater solidity on psychiatric identity, which is then distinguished by its difference from the old system. With the passage of time, however, these revolutionary innovations have in general lost much of their attraction or proved valid only for specific, more or less limited fields of mental suffering. The archetypal psychiatric revolution, beyond any possible doubt, was the institution of the asylum; but it was only with Pinel's revolutionary gesture in freeing the lunatics from their chains at the Bicêtre Hospital in 1793 that therapeutic prospects, as opposed to mere repression, emerged. Just under two centuries later, the life of the big mental hospital had run its course; instead of appearing as a support for therapy, it came to be seen as a source of superimposed pathologies (institutionalisation)

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and chaos theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and chaos theory

Ferdinand Verhulst

Ferdinand Verhulst

PO Box 80.0 10
3508 TA Utrecht
The Netherlands

Ferdinand Verhulst  

Dear Sir,

In volume 78 of your Journal there appeared an interesting paper by Jean-Michel Quinodoz (1997) on psychoanalytic phenomena and concepts and the new possibilities for describing these in terms of chaos theory. The latter is a branch of nonlinear dynamics which is very mathematical in language and methods and which has already many amazing new applications in the natural sciences.

There are many important observations in this paper which are useful to discuss but here I would like to follow up on a remark on page 716: ‘If psychoanalysts have learned much from scientists, might it not also be the scientists' turn to cast an occasional glance in the direction of the psychoanalysts?’ I feel this request is phrased in such a modest way that it can hardly be refused; in fact, inspired by reading Moran (1991) and Vann Spruiell (1993) I wrote a paper (Verhulst, 1994) which is an attempt to do just that. I intend to study in more detail the interesting relationships between psychoanalysis and chaos theory but in this letter I want to restrict myself to a rather fundamental, methodological question which arises out of the paper by Quinodoz (1997).

The question is this: if one uses concepts of chaos theory like ‘strange attractors’ to describe ‘unconscious fantasies’ in psychoanalysis or, as I intend to do in a subsequent paper, to use the dynamics of ‘intermittency’ to describe the evolution of ‘a split personality’, has this description then the status of a model or a metaphor?

Quinodoz answers this question by stating that ‘these ideas open up new perspectives, extending beyond mere metaphors …’ and he labels such comparisons (for instance strange attractor-unconscious fantasy) as ‘analogy-type model’. I disagree and I feel this is not just a semantic question. The way we look at these comparisons is fundamental for this type of concept formation in psychoanalysis and also for the perspectives psychoanalysis has in its relations with the natural and the other social sciences. I will start explaining this with a citation from my (1994)

responseSet.title: The Phenomenon of Object-Presenting and its Implications for Development
responseSet.authorMast: Vera Regina J. R. M. Fonseca
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Phenomenon of Object-Presenting and its Implications for Development

Vera Fonseca

Vera Fonseca

Al. Jauaperi 1413
04523-010 São Paulo
vlfonseca@jvr.org.br

Vera Fonseca  

On the basis of her clinical experience with psychotic children who suffer from severe difficulties in tolerating the autonomous existence of the external object, the author discusses Winnicott's concept of object-presenting, asking what the prerequisites are for infants' acceptance and use of the object; that is, for its otherness to be assimilated. An attempt is made to establish a connection between Winnicott's and Klein's ideas on this subject, and the possible importance of such issues in normal development is indicated. Three examples of psychotic children are used to illustrate in detail several manoeuvres for avoiding the perception of the not-me, in the pattern of thinking that Tustin examined so well. After the clinical discussion, the author concludes that some children may be innately more susceptible to reacting negatively to the perception of the caretaker's otherness, and that such a characteristic combines with the environmental conditions, particularly to the style of maternal care, to produce or prevent further pathology. The rhythm, the imitative games, the gradual and partial presentation of the external object, and also certain possible methods of controlling the object, seem to belong to the set of maternal ‘techniques’ that are used spontaneously during the interactions. This style can also be useful at times in psychoanalytical work with severely disturbed children.

responseSet.title: Otto Gross
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Otto Gross

Gottfried Heuer

Gottfried Heuer

13 Mansell Rd.
London W3 7QH
gottfried.heuer@virgin.net

Gottfried Heuer  

Dear Sir,

I am writing to you because some information about my recent research might be of interest for the readers of your journal.

Although the Austrian psychoanalyst Otto Gross (1977–1920) played a pivotal role in the birth of what today we are calling modernity, with wide-ranging influences in analysis, psychiatry, literature, and sociology, he has remained virtually unknown to this day. To a large extent, this is the result of an analytic historiography which Erich Fromm has rightly called ‘Stalinistic’ (Fromm, 1989, p. 195): dissidents become non-persons and vanish from the records.

Yet there was a time, in the first decades of this century, when the greatest minds in analysis were full of the highest praise for Otto Gross. In 1908 Freud wrote to Jung, ‘You are really the only one capable of making an original contribution; except perhaps O. Gross’ (Freudl Jung Letters, p. 126, ed. McGuire, 1974). A few months later, after his mutual analysis with Gross, Jung replied to Freud, ‘In Gross I discovered many aspects of my own nature, so that he often seemed like my twin brother’ (p. 156). In 1910 Ferenczi wrote to Freud about Gross, ‘There is no doubt that, among those who have followed you up to now, he is the most significant’ (Correspondence Freudl Ferenczi, Vol. I, p. 154, ed. Brabant et al., 1993). In 1912 Alfred Adler referred to Gross as ‘brilliant’ (Adler, 1997, p. 58). Both Karl Abraham and Ferenczi reviewed Gross' works (Abraham, 1905; Ferenczi, 1920). Wilhelm Stekel spoke of the ‘ingenious Otto Gross’ (Stekel, 1923, p. 464). And in Free Associations, the autobiography Ernest Jones was working on at the end of his life, he wrote, Gross ‘was my first instructor in the technique of psychoanalysis’ and called him ‘the nearest approach to the romantic ideal of a genius I have ever met’ (Jones, 1959, p. 173f).

One generation before Wilhelm Reich, Otto Gross was the first analyst to emphasise the dialectical interdependence between individual inner change on the one hand and collective political change on the other. In 1913 he wrote, ‘The psychology of the unconscious is the philosophy of the revolution’ (Gross, 1913, col. 385). Gross had a lasting influence on Freud, Jung and other leading analysts. When Freud referred Gross to Jung for analysis in 1908, he wrote to the latter why he himself had not wanted to take on Gross as a patient: ‘the difficulty would have been that the dividing line between our respective property rights in creative ideas would inevitably have been effaced; we would never have been able to disentangle them with a clear conscience’ (Freudl Jung Letters, p. 152). Gross did influence the course of analytic theory and clinical practice to the present day. He was a ‘pioneer of life experiment’ (Green, 1998, Introduction) and tried to live his radical ideas both in his private and his professional life—which he refused to separate. Thus he became unacceptable to those trying to establish the credibility of analysis as a science in the eyes of society and academe in the early years of this century. Already in 1921, less than a year after his death, the writer Anton Kuh wrote of Gross as ‘a man known only to very few by name—apart from a handful of psychiatrists and secret policemen—and among those few only to those who plucked his feathers to adorn their own posteriors’ (Kuh, 1921, pp. 161ff.)

[[RunningHead]]

Dicionário Comentado do Alemão de Freud [An annotated dictionary of Freud's German terms]

Luiz Alberto Hanns. Rio de Janeiro: Imago. 1997. Pp. 506

Renato Mezan

Renato Mezan

R. Amália de Noronha 198
05410-010 São Paulo

Renato Mezan  

Luiz Hanns directs his work to two kinds of Freud scholars. The first, those not knowing German, will have access to a layer of Freud's text that is not normally found in translations. The second type of reader may know enough German to understand the meaning of the original but will not be aware of the associations that the central terms of each phrase will have for the native speaker.

Hanns, who is a Germanist and psychoanalyst, has published an outstanding work. The Dictionary presents and discusses some of the most difficult psychoanalytical terms. Translation problems presented by those terms are common for several Latin languages (French, Spanish, Italian and Portuguese) as well as for English psychoanalytical language in which Greek and Latin terms are frequently employed. In the original Brazilian edition each German term is compared with the standard Brazilian translations. A Spanish edition will be published during the first part of 1999, in which each German term will be compared to the usual Spanish translations. An English version will also be prepared.

Hanns chose forty important theoretical and psychoanalytical terms, and the book consists of a detailed commentary on each of them (Angst, Abwehr, Bearbeitung, Einfall, Darstellen, etc., to Trieb and Übertragung). Each entry has six sections: (1) the term in German; (2) its etymology and correlated terms; (3) a comparison with the term in Portuguese,3 (4) examples of Freud's use of this term; (5) additional commentaries. This form of procedure is based on a principle that is taken from linguistics: that a specific German word has only an approximate correspondence to its equivalent in other languages. There is a common semantic nucleus, but the German term may have connotations that are not present in Portuguese, while the Portuguese word may also have additional meanings that are absent from its German equivalent.

One example will clarify the value of this method: that of the term Besetzung (investment or cathexis). Hanns begins by showing that the connotations of this word ‘are connected to the idea of mobility and fluidity, which is pertinent to the Freudian model of the circulation of energy, where there are ways, a locus, fixations and liberations, entrances and exits, blocks, etc.’ (p. 89). He then enumerates the dictionary meanings of the word, giving examples:

occupy a place (‘the toilet is occupied’)

responseSet.title: Midwifery of the Soul: A Holistic Perspective on Psychoanalysis: Margaret Arden. London: Free Association Books. 1998. Pp. 118
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Midwifery of the Soul: A Holistic Perspective on Psychoanalysis

Margaret Arden. London: Free Association Books. 1998. Pp. 118

Helen Robinson

Helen Robinson

4, Whellock Road, London W4 1DZ

Helen Robinson  

This is a collection of eight papers that gives special consideration and place to the following idea:

Ever splitting the light!

How often do they strive to divide

that which, despite everything, would always

remain single and whole.

This quotation, set before the contents page of the collection, is from Goethe.

It informs all the thinking behind Margaret Arden's distinct and separately driven subjects for this collection, and it makes possible the disparateness of her titles and themes for exploration, precisely because it acts as the anchor and unitary base for separateness and difference.

In this sense, she locates vastly different-seeming matter within this central thought expressed by Goethe, the whole that holds and affirms against the dissection that misapprehends in the interest of detail. It is in my view essential that our acts of desecration in the interests of our own brand of understanding we call science and knowledge should have this salutary prescript, this reminder of the importance of the overriding whole, in order that we may be safe from our own undoing of science and meaning. It is timely, at the close of the first century of psychoanalysis. It is both a statement about the position of psychoanalysis, or that of any other human discipline, come to that, for us to attend to seriously and also the affirmation that whatever we may believe and hope we achieve through the workings of splitting and dividing and tabulating methodically Goethe's ‘Nature’, in other words, ‘psychoanalysing’ the elemental forces will always assert their position to the contrary.

Those that speak, as the author does in these papers, in honour of those disparate forces and their unity, not sameness but ways of expressing difference within the unity, will act in the care and best interests of both psychoanalysis, or any other human discipline, and Nature, of which man is a single part.

Holism, then, is the driving force of these writings, which are also a developmental or processive journey for this one adventurous and exploratory psychoanalyst, and to those for whom psychoanalysis needs no such positioning or placing within a framework, these essays may seem tangential, at an angle to their work, nevertheless interesting.

[[RunningHead]]

Naisen Verhottu Sisin [The veiled female core]

Elina Mäenpää-Reenkola: Helsinki: Helsinki University Press. 1997. Pp. 89

Riitta Sirola

Riitta Sirola

Jääkärinkatu 5 B 3, 00150 Helsinki

Riitta Sirola  

The focus of this book lies in the impact of a woman's body on her psyche. According to Elina Mäenpää-Reenkola, a little girl uses her body as a source of pleasure and enjoyment from early on. Maternal desire, touch and embrace awaken the girl's ability to be satisfied enough with herself and her body and to trust herself. Her increasing confidence paves the way to the role of mother, of sexually active woman and a man's companion, but also to her contribution as an independent, creative individual. In these roles, a woman may experience conflicting feelings of lethargy and aggression, which can easily remain unspoken since they arouse strong feelings of guilt and shame.

620

Épître à l'objet [Epistle to the object]

Florence Guignard. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. 1997. Pp. 173

D. Arnoux

D. Arnoux

5, rue de l'Essai, 75005 Paris

D. Arnoux  

The sense of identity is a theme that pervades Florence Guignard's book like Ariadne's thread. The author takes a strictly Freudian view of sexuality and the drives. Following her previous book (Au vif de l'infantile. Lausanne: Delachaux et Niestlé, 1996), she now offers the reader a bold and stimulating metapsychological survey. The underlying inspiration of this interesting volume lies in the fundamental issues of the contemporary psychoanalytic debate.

For the author, the object is a ‘fleeting’—i.e. ‘polysemous’—concept.

Le propre de l'homme: Psychanalyse et préhistoire [The specificity of man: psychoanalysis and prehistory]

François Sacco and Georges Sauvet. Lausanne: Delachaux et Niestlé (Champs Psychanalytiques). 1998. Pp. 202

Françoise Bouchard

Françoise Bouchard

19 avenue Théophile Gautier, 75016 Paris

Françoise Bouchard  

This book, the fruit of a twenty-year association between psychoanalysts and prehistorians, seeks to establish the basis of the singularity of man. It includes contributions by the psychoanalysts Alain Gibeault and Richard Uhl, Marie-Lise Roux and François Sacco (all members of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society) and by the prehistorians Georges Sauvet, Gilles Tosello, Yvette Taborin, Denis Vialou and Jean-Pierre Mohen.

The following quotation from Freud's The Interpretation of Dreams (1900, S.E. 4-5), reproduced on p. 42, in itself provides sufficient justification for this co-operation between prehistorians and analysts:

Dreams and neuroses seem to have preserved more mental antiquities than we could have imagined possible; so that psycho-analysis may claim a high place among the sciences which are concerned with the re-construction of the earliest and most obscure periods of the beginnings of the human race.

Psychoanalysts and prehistorians, ‘united in the search for the origins and meaning of their finds’, cause ‘the remains of bygone epochs’ to speak. In this way, the nine authors make the reader ponder over what it is that characterises the human. Each provides a ‘definition’ of the specificity of man in accordance with his or her own field of specialisation and scientific interests.

For the psychoanalysts Alain Gibeault and Richard Uhl, what is specific to man is the compulsion to symbolise. Presenting theoretical arguments in favour of their conception, they show how man constantly strives towards a goal, which demands a ‘representation’ (see glossary, p. 201) and objects thereby represented. The authors note at the same time that, when the hands were freed by bipedalism, the first hominids became capable, unlike animals, of creating tools to fashion other tools (homo habilis, 2.5 million to 1.5 million years ago). This spectacular specialisation was accompanied by an evolution from biological rhythms to socialisation. According to Leroi-Gourhan (1964, personal communcation), language arose contemporaneously with tool-making ability. And language is inseparable from the capacity to symbolise. Alain Gibeault shares the prehistorians' conception of the ‘creative rhythms of space and time’ and of forms, whose origins he traces back to the rhythmic alternation of pleasure and unpleasure. Hence the generation of figurative rhythms, culminating in the capacity of homo sapiens to create images (35,000 years ago)

[[RunningHead]]

Fundamentos narcisistas y espejos rotos: reflexiones sobre el narcisismo [Narcissistic fundamentals and broken mirrors: reflections on narcissism]

Pedro Guillem Nácher. Valencia: Editorial Promolibro. 1996. Pp. 205

Raul Vilanova

Raul Vilanova

Avenida Nazaret 6, 28009 Madrid

Raul Vilanova  

The author introduces his book as ‘yet another of the many devoted to this subject’—i.e. narcissism. It is divided into three parts. The first deals with the different versions of the myth of Narcissus and certain features the author believes it shares with that of Oedipus, the other primordial myth of psychoanalytic literature. He argues that Oedipus and Narcissus ‘have their origins in a violent primal scene, and both are unwanted children (unwanted, at least, by their two parents) who were abducted by force’ (p. 28). The fact that this is the sum total of our information on the infantile history of Narcissus—unlike the situation with Oedipus—reminds the author of the ‘histories full of silence and fury’ (p. 30) observed in certain narcissistic pathologies. However, a deeper sense underlies the similarities and differences between the two stories, which, for Guillem, are ‘linked … by initiation myths that lay down rules and precepts, taboos and prohibitions, associated with the transition from childhood to adulthood’ (p. 30). These thought-provoking ideas initially suggest an attempt to connect concepts that are divergent if not antagonistic in psychoanalysis, namely narcissism and the Oedipus complex. This, though, is not the tenor of the book.

The second part begins with a chronological review of the notion of narcissism in Freud's oeuvre, in which Guillem draws attention to the presence of many contradictions. The author's survey of Freud is based on three main considerations: the relationship between auto-erotism and narcissism, the relationship between narcissism and the concept of the ego (as well as that of the self) and the links between the theory of narcissism and the second drive theory. This is a reading of Freud based on a familiarity with object-relations theory and incorporating the contributions not only of Winnicott and Bion but also of Laplanche and Green.

The author continues with a discussion of common elements in and differences between a number of authors, whom he distinguishes not by geographical schools but according to whether their ideas make use of Freud's second theory of the drives (Pasche, Bion, Green, Rosenfeld and Kernberg) or not (Tausk, Federn, Grunberger, Mahler, Kohut and Laplanche, as well as Bowlby and Balint, and implicitly also Winnicott and Fairbairn). Rather than selecting the views with which Guillem identifies, I shall review his own ideas, which he goes on to present on the basis of a rereading of Freud's unfinished Outline of Psycho-Analysis (1940, S.E. 23).

[[RunningHead]]

Schizophrenia. Its Origins and Need-Adapted Treatment

Yrjo O. Alanen. London: Karnac Books. 1997. Pp. 312

Richard Lucas

Richard Lucas

63 Ossulton Way, London N2 0JY

Richard Lucas  

This book describes forty years of psychotherapeutic involvement with schizophrenic patients in Finland by Professor Alanen. Its aim is the promotion of a concept and therapy based on an integrated psychodynamic approach. His organisational and research abilities resulted in the creation of the Turku Schizophrenia Project, followed by the Finnish National Schizophrenia Project and an Inter-Scandinavian Project.

In his foreword, Stephen Fleck describes the book as the most comprehensive treatise on schizophrenia in twenty-five years, and notes the way in which Alanen's aura of a truly caring physician pervades the book.

In the first chapter, Alanen describes his early contact with schizophrenic patients through visits with his father, a municipal health officer in a rural community in Finland. He comments on the crucial importance of his own analysis, begun in 1955, in helping develop his approach to psychotic patients. As illustration, he gives a moving vignette from his early pre-analytic days in which he felt impelled by a patient to stroke her hair. The patient responded with: ‘Yrjo Alanen is speaking; he has become my guide’.

Alanen uncomfortably withdrew to ordinary conversation but reflected that with his later analytic experience, he would have contained the countertransference feelings to comfort the patient and addressed the delusional transference.

He does, however, criticise both that ‘classical psycho-analytic therapy has engendered a pessimistic attitude towards psychotherapeutic treatment of schizophrenics’ (p. 114) and that training programmes are restricted to experiences with less disturbed patients.

In a chapter reviewing illness models and different treatment approaches to schizophrenia, Alanen argues that all the approaches—biomedical, individual, familial, therapeutic community and rehabilitative—are justified, but that treatment should be based on a case-specific need. Following a masterly condensed review of the literature on organic aspects, hereditary factors, psychoanalytic and psychodynamic studies, Alanen presents his own integrated model for the development of schizophrenia in a schematic form modified from Ciompi's work.

Ruth Lax  

A sense of frustration and deprivation in the analysand is frequently caused by the extraordinary privacy of the analytic situation, its ‘imbalance’ because the analysand divulges—to different degrees—her/his intrasubjective experiences, the subjectively known ‘history’ and perceived outer reality, while the analyst keeps silent about his own experiences and reactions. Complaints about how this reality of the analytic situation is considered are familiar, as is the frequently extreme curiosity of the analysand. Struggles about these issues, their meaning and significance are known only to the two participants. They are also the ones who know what took place, even though the intra- and intersubjective reality of each usually differs.

The editors of The Inward Eye, both analysts, acknowledge in the Introduction (p. xiii)

Klaus Fink  

Dear Sir,

I read this interesting paper by Marilyn Charles (Int. J. Psychoanal., 1999, 80: 783-95) and could not refrain from commenting on the author's ingenuity at applying bi-logic to a clinical paper of old that surely has been reviewed and quoted innumerable times. But also Marilyn Charles uses Matte-Blanco's conceptualisations to understand and most of all to clarify her own clinical case Michael, presented in her paper. Apart from my own published papers, this is for me the first time that I see a straightforward clinical paper in which Matte-Blanco is used to understand psychological mechanisms at work. The author uses the concepts of symmetry and asymmetry to explain what she sees as happening in the exchanges between Winnicott and his patient and also between herself and her patient. A certain amount of work has been published about bi-logic but it mostly consists in theoretical lucubrations about it. The clinical use of bi-logic in understanding of what goes on in a session, the interplay of transference and countertransference, of projection, introjection, projective identification,

splitting, repression and so on has been scarce. And yet, these psychic mechanisms can be made very clear by the use of Matte-Blanco's concepts and it is precisely this which Marilyn Charles stresses in her paper on page 789. There she talks about her patient Michael, whom she helps to ‘desymmetrise’ his way of thinking about his own child and himself. What I hope will not be overlooked, and that is my reason for writing this comment, is what Marilyn Charles says at the bottom of page 792. Here she stresses the use she has made of Matte-Blanco's terminology to make sense of what goes on in her patient's mind. I agree entirely with her when she says that bi-logic is not a new psychoanalytical theory but a more succinct and clear way of formulating and understanding psychic mechanisms, whether they are formulated in Freudian, Kleinian, Middle, ego-psychological or any other theoretical form.

Final Over View Panel

Leonardo Wender and Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser

Buenos Aires

3 Blackfan Circle
Boston
MA 02115-5794

Stuart Hauser  

In opening this final panel the Chairman commented on the richness of the entire Congress. He invited each of the three presenters to offer their perspectives on the varied presentations and overall experience of this Congress. The first panellist, Elizabeth Tabak de Bianchedi, began her comments with a theme that characterised the succeeding two panellists, namely the near-impossibility of synthesising all that was said and done with 2,000 participants, five days of plenary sessions, additional panels and numerous other presentations. She decided to present the idea that this was a ‘polymorphic’ Congress. Certain aspects of sexuality were accentuated within the Congress while others were taken less clearly into account.

De Bianchedi went on to offer a range of meanings of polymorphism with respect to the Congress, including: the wealth of aesthetic and historical periods to be seen in Barcelona's architecture; the range of forty-two countries represented at this Congress; the use of four official languages at our meetings; the participation of both senior and junior members of the profession; the array of fields of study including philosophy, biology, analyses of adults, analyses of children, studies of teenagers and gay and lesbian analyses; the broad range of topics presented including types of analysis and psychotherapy of transvestites, of perversions, aspects of sexuality related to cultural variables and medical diagnostic considerations, debates about biological determinism, psychoanalytic dimensions, Darwinism and many other interrelated fields; the host of psychoanalytic theories considered, including Freud, Klein, Winnicott, Kohut, Kernberg and Reich as well as post-Freudian schools and working groups; the variety of terms considered, such as castration, sexuality, identity and gender as well as the even broader ones such as masculinity, femininity and gender; the many ways in which the papers themselves were disseminated; the multitude of new notions such as erotic chains, virtual fetishes and subliminal language; and the significant attention to the artistic, including music, tone, voice, melody, painting, art, the work of Picasso, Goethe and Cervantes, papers about Japanese art, and a seminar addressing Gaudi's work.

Response

Michael Trupp

Michael Trupp

969 Park Avenue
New York
NY 10028

Michael Trupp  

Dear Sir,

While it would require an odd mentality indeed to be ‘dismissive of philosophy generally,’ formal applications of philosophy to clinical psychoanalysis remain—after a century or so—highly problematic. (See Merleau-Ponty's cogent comments on Freud's ‘pitiless hermeneutic’ 1983.) Serious students of psychoanalysis should, I suggest, remain alert to smug and fatuous anti-Freudian tracts packaged as scholarly discourse.

In her, perhaps too hasty, reading of my review, Cavell may have overlooked the subtitle of the book, The Psychoanalytic Unconscious and its Philosophical Critics, as well as my acknowledgment of Levy's appropriate objections to these ‘critic's' most glaring distoritions. (I do feel that Levy often seems to praise his ‘targets’ with faint damns.)

Cavell might also note that ‘these days’ most of the philosophers here in question are indeed contemporary i.e., alive and, well, and I imagine, ‘productive.’ For example, at a recent meeting of the New York Psychoanalytic Society, Grunbaum espoused his total lack of ‘hands on’ experience with psychoanalysis as an advantageous spur to his ‘objectivity.’ Relatedly, Cavell's plea for a potentially sappy ecumenicism should remind us of the ‘successful’ aborting of the first Library of Congress Freud exhibit by ‘colleagues in neighboring disciplines.’

171

Geoffrey Baruch  

Dear Sir,

Dr Furman writes that I was ignorant at the time of the treatment of the role of the parents in all aspects of my young patient's life including his analysis. I find it impossible to understand, on the basis of the text, how Dr Furman arrives at this view. From the outset, I refer without contradiction to the referring agency's assessment that there were considerable emotional difficulties between the patient and his mother and to the arrangements made for the parents to work with a member of the referring agency's staff. In my account, I take into consideration the complex role and needs of the patient's parents and their bearing on my young patient and his analysis. Unfortunately, for unexpected reasons, the support offered to the parents in this case broke down and could not be resurrected. The course the analysis subsequently took underlines the need for parental support

Yours sincerely

172

Donald Spence  

Dear Sir,

I want to thank Dr Burland for his thoughtful reply to my editorial and for his clarification of his initial observations. What he was claiming —that his sample of some 450 cases manifested ‘almost without exception … a step-by-step regression into a revisitation of their past, in memory, in the transference, and in current reality …’ (Burland, 1997, p. 469)—seemed almost too good to be true; hence my comment. But if, on the other hand, it is true, it would provide impressive evidence for a general theory of psychoanalytic action and the data cries out to be made public.

Two questions arise almost immediately. First, how can we check the validity of the reports by the candidates and second, how can we test Dr Burland's observation that each case followed the same regressive sequence? I see no direct answer to the first but it might be possible to ask independent judges, with similar training and experience, to review each of the summaries and rate them on the degree to which they conformed to Dr Burland's model. If a high degree of concordance was found, it would not only reinforce Dr Burland's initial impression but would increase the likelihood that the candidates' reports had faithfully captured their clinical work. This last point follows from the assumption that it would be highly unlikely that the candidates were aware of Dr Burland's healing sequence at the time they wrote up their cases (and he himself, as we know, was surprised by the commonality).

If the sample survives these tests, then we have greater reason to believe that the concordance observed by Dr Burland is more than one man's opinion. We can then proceed to ask more searching questions of the data and learn, in more detail, just how the pattern of working through is related, in each case, to therapeutic action and psychoanalytic outcome. Does this inherent process of self-healing show itself in all successful cases? If broken off prematurely, does it lead to treatment failure? Does the analyst sometimes get in the way and prevent the process from taking its natural course? Does the sequence appear regardless of the analyst's orientation?

Chaya H. Roth and Steven D. Kulb. Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press. 1997. Pp. 308

Ilene Lefcourt

Ilene Lefcourt

1051 Riverside Drive, New York, NY 10032

Ilene Lefcourt  

Although the relevance of infant observation to psychoanalysis has been argued about extensively, there seems to be less controversy about the relevance of psychoanalysis to the study, or treatment, of infants. Selma Fraiberg, Daniel Stern, Bertrand Cramer, Beatrice Beebe and others have described the application of psychoanalytic theory to parent-infant psychotherapy and have ushered in a renewed interest in psychoanalytically informed treatments of infants and parents. With the increased number of infant clinical services and training programmes, the relevance of psychoanalysis to infant treatment warrants further attention. In The Multiple Facets of Ther-apeutic Transactions, Roth & Kulb present a theory of psychological functioning and therapeutic intervention that assumes both the relevance of psychoanalysis to the treatment of infants and the relevance of infant observation to psychoanalysis. Having experienced the compelling nature of infant clinical work, their personal convictions permeate the book. Their assumptions of relevance, without discussion, supporting empirical findings or clinical data weaken their argument.

Roth & Kulb attempt to formulate a ‘universal’ theory of psychological functioning (p. vii). Their theory is based on observations of parent-infant interaction made during their sixteen years of clinical and training experience at the University of Chicago Parent Infant Development Service. Concerned that no single existing theory is acceptable to all clinicians or applicable to all patients, they attempt to bring together aspects of several theoretical perspectives by conceptualising them as four facets of psychological functioning: interpersonal, developmental, characterological and intrapsychic. They further subdivide these facets into domains, aims, conservative tendencies, assumptions and principles. The overarching principal of their model is transactionalism. They redefine familiar psychoanalytic concepts, ‘for the sake of clarifying the purported transactions within and across the domains and aims of the facets of the interpersonal and intrapersonal context’ (p. 20). For example, ‘the concept of id … becomes that aspect of self that constitutes the I-process …’ (p. 20). They ‘elaborate’ the concept of character armour in order to describe, ‘the development of functions that provide an envelope that guards and informs character and self which interweave inextricably in development’ (p. 17). In order to ‘expand’ the notion of defence from an intrapsychic mechanism into an interpersonal transaction, they reformulate it in terms of a coping strategy.

responseSet.title: Inre värld och förandring [Inner world and change]: Ludvig Igra. Stockholm: Natur och Kultur. 1996. Pp. 241
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Inre värld och förandring [Inner world and change]

Ludvig Igra. Stockholm: Natur och Kultur. 1996. Pp. 241

Merete Katznelson

Merete Katznelson

Nerievej 3, DK-2900 Hellerup, Denmark

Merete Katznelson  

Ludvig Igra is a training analyst of the Swedish Society of Holistic Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy. His first two books, Object Relations and Psychotherapy (1983) and A Question of Life and Death (1988) [my translation], discussed the decisive influence of object relations on human development and the relation between narcissism and human destructivity, respectively.

In his new book Inner World and Change, the author further explores concepts such as self-organisation, inner objects and identification as well as the unconscious dynamic conditions that promote or hinder change in the inner world. The book is part of a widespread interest in psychic change as a goal for psychoanalytic treatment.

Igra does not claim to present a comprehensive theory of change. His way of thinking is close to Kleinian thinking as it has been described by Steiner (1996, ‘The aim of psychoanalysis in theory and in practice’ Int. J. Psychoanal., 77: 1073–1083). He examines the function and position of inner objects seen in the light of projective identification and examines those interventions that promote or inhibit change.

As an image of the process of change he turns to the Biblical description of the Exodus of the Jews from the land of Egypt. Exodus describes the deliverance from slavery and captivity and the long journey full of dangers through the desert to the promised land. This journey across the desert from slavery to the promised land gives rise to the constant interactions between the anxiety of human beings to leave a known environment and their endeavours to reach new goals. In these efforts human beings can be seized by unbearable feelings of loneliness, which threaten their movement forward and make them resort to inexpedient solutions.

Thus when Moses lingers on the mountain his people feel abandoned. They lament on their difficulties and contemplate going back to Egypt, to slavery. In this state of abandonment, anxiety and insecurity they create their own god, a golden calf that they can control and worship. They gain control over their anxiety, they idolise their new god but they do not realise that he is dead.

responseSet.title: The Remains of the Day
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Remains of the Day

Mechthild Zeul

Mechthild Zeul

Pza. de la Cuidad de Viena 6, Apto. 1017
28040 Madrid

Mechthild Zeul  

The Remains of the Day, 1993

Directed by James Ivory

Distributed by Columbia Tristar Home Video

Freud felt that it was only possible to develop cogent and definitive conclusions from the clinical material of a psychoanalytic treatment after termination. In a similar vein, only after I had watched The Remains of the Day several times and had the entire film text in front of my inner eye was I able to develop some tentative interpretive formulations about the film. My basic methodology for approaching this film lies in the assumption that there is an exchange between the film and its interpretive audience that manifests itself in a network of multiple identifications. Reflections on these identifications reveal a direct connection to psychoanalytic theory. A new text is created that is anchored in the concrete data of the film but no longer resides exclusively or directly in the film images. Instead, the new text grows out of the psychoanalytic interpretation brought to bear on the film.

There is a parallel situation in the narrative of the film itself. Closely integrated are the collaboration of Lord Darlington with the Nazi regime and the unhappy love story between the housekeeper, Ms Kenton, and the butler, Stevens. The film deftly sketches the interrelationship of these two threads of action through a psychological portrait of the protagonist Stevens and his authoritarian, contemptuous behaviour towards others. In essence, The Remains of the Day is a social criticism film that depicts psychopathological preconditions for the socially recognised behaviour of an English butler. He is neutral towards the political convictions and moral values of his master—knowledge of them would greatly impede the fulfilment of his purpose in life, which is to serve loyally and unobtrusively. His one and only task is to carry out his master's orders and wishes in exquisite detail and without any reservations whatsoever.

Being of service has an inherent value for Stevens. The film persuasively depicts him as completely out of touch with his own feelings and thoughts, suggesting that in serving his master, he loses contact with himself. The object master has displaced his ego ideal. The tragedy of the film, though, is that through the willing internalisation of this object into his ego, he then depends on its physical and psychic presence to sustain meaning in his life or even life itself. Without explicitly stating this conclusion, the film certainly allows for an interpretive understanding that blind obedience to one's master through the internalisation of the honoured and valued object may lead to a transcendent form of narcissistic fulfilment. The master then completes the self in the sense of Kohut's description of the selfobject. As the film unfolds, the audience gradually reaches the chilling conclusion that internalisation and blind obedience of this nature may have been essential prerequisites for the amoral behaviour of the Germans that made possible the genocide of the Jews.

responseSet.title: ‘What is “applied” in “applied” psychoanalysis?’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘What is “applied” in “applied” psychoanalysis?’

Bruce Levin

Bruce Levin

15 St Asaph's Road
PO Box 630
Bala Cynwyd
PA 19004
DrBLevin@aol.com

Bruce Levin  

Dear Sir,

Dr Aaron Esman's excellent work ‘What is “applied” in “applied” psychoanalysis?’ (Int. J. Psychoanal., 79: 741-756) incisively cuts through the prejudices against scientific approaches to applied psychoanalysis. His scholarly, well-researched study beautifully demonstrates the point that integrating ‘psychoanalytic institutes and centres into the larger world of the academy … would promote the effective teaching of psychoanalysis as a basic conceptual discipline for students in many fields’ (p. 749). But he concludes that this goal may be too difficult to attain given the current economic and intellectual climates.

An article which so skilfully challenges conventional thinking does not need to conclude with these conventional concerns, particularly since this conclusion flies in the face of fact.

At the Philadelphia Association for Psychoanalysis we have established a Psychoanalytic Fellowship Program which includes academics and non-clinicians. The Fellowship Program is an academic honour bestowed upon appropriate applicants through our Institute. This cost-effective initiative involves the Fellows in monthly meetings with Psychoanalytic Mentors selected from our faculty, who help the Fellows apply psychoanalytic concepts to their professions. The Fellows are also invited to attend classes in our Psychotherapy Training Program and Scientific Sessions. In only four years we have attracted Professors in English, Bioethics, History, Philosophy, a District Attorney and Public Defender, primary care physicians, a sociologist, several educators, an owner and director of a highly regarded art gallery, and a respected mental health journalist.

This accomplishment has been economical and has diversified teaching and learning throughout our organisation, yielding a renewed sense of mission and morale. I agree with Dr Esman that incorporating academicians in psychoanalytic institutes, ‘remains not only worth pursuing but, perhaps, a condition for survival’ (p. 749).

In the spirit of Dr Esman's challenge to conventional thinking about applied psychoanalysis and in light of our experience in Philadelphia, I can only suggest: ‘Build it and they shall come’.

Raija Pohjamo  

Tor-Björn Hägglund is a pioneer of psychoanalysis for adolescents in Finland, and has been in clinical co-operation with Raimo Hyttinen for twenty years. This book is a necessary new addition to the texts written on the psychoanalysis of young people. The writers' long and wide experience of adolescence, its growth and development, based on practice and research is conveyed to the reader from all three parts of the book: I ‘Growth during adolescence’, II ‘Special features of psychoanalysis for adolescents’, and III ‘Three case studies’. The authors suggest that in his or her external behaviour, the young person creates an interaction with the environment that resembles his or her past in detail. It is like a play telling of the hidden experience of his most difficult traumas, and on the other hand of the early germs of his actual growth.

In Part I the authors discuss the acting-out behaviour of young people and go briefly through the normative crises: breaking loose from the bonds of childhood, creation of the sexual body, and creation of a personal future. The development starts in the sphere of youth culture, from which the young person can create a generation gap. This is done where the soil is safe. The adolescent also needs support from the outside to be able to organise his growth process around the Oedipus complex. In one of his earlier books, (1990, Oidipus iänikuinen

responseSet.title: Why did Freud Reject God? A Psychoanalytic Interpretation: Ana-Maria Rizzuto. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press. 1998. Pp. 297
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Why did Freud Reject God? A Psychoanalytic Interpretation

Ana-Maria Rizzuto. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press. 1998. Pp. 297

Paul Schwaber

Paul Schwaber

258 Bradley Street
New Haven, CT 06511

Paul Schwaber  

Looking hard and long at the Philippson Bible, with which Jakob Freud delighted and instructed his little boy, Ana-Maria Rizzuto came to a realisation. A remarkable number of pieces in Sigmund Freud's collection of antiquities closely resembled illustrations in that Bible. Freud's antiquities famously crowded his office. Other than attesting to archaeology as a master metaphor of his thought, the collection had no organising theme that scholars discerned—beyond, that is, his pleasure in the pieces and persisting commitment to collecting them. Arrayed in tight orderliness on his shelves, table-tops and writing desk, the antiquities proved the silent companions of his psychoanalytic work. The largest number were Egyptian; the bias of the Philippson illustrations was too. What if those illustrations from childhood, by unconscious visual memory, fuelled Freud's adult collecting? The perception had promise, and fascinating ramifications, which Rizzuto explores with lovely subtlety in this original and important book.

Freud began to buy ancient artefacts just after his father's death. Thus the collection was launched concurrently with his self-analysis and the discoveries that led to The Interpretation of Dreams (1900). Manifestly, although less obviously than the remarkable technique, observation, theory-building and writing, the antiquities were associated with his intense and tumultuous after-shock and sustained, creative sublimation that ensued upon his father's death. But how? If really part of the story, what part?

Rizzuto weaves both the antiquities and the family Bible into the pattern she traces of Freud's

rejection of God. It was a process that took conscious hold in adolescence, wavered briefly when he studied philosophy with Brentano at the university of Vienna, and was consolidated in a complicated fashion when his father died. Once, she reminds us, God had a positive valence for him, when his nurse took him as a toddler to her church and he would return to sermonise his parents about God's power and glory. Eventually, to be sure, Freud would quip to Pfister about psychoanalysis being founded by ‘a godless Jew’ and dismiss religion as a neurotic illusion of mankind. What intrigues Rizzuto, the author of an excellent book on the psychology of belief and unbelief (1979), is how Freud's early God-internalisations transformed. She explores the developmental dynamics of his atheism.

[[RunningHead]]

The Elusive Human Subject

Roger Kennedy. London and New York: Free Association Books. 1998. Pp. 220

Olivier Flournoy

Olivier Flournoy

45, avenue de Champel, 1206 Geneva

Olivier Flournoy  

When the International Journal of Psychoanalysis asked me to review Roger Kennedy's book, little did I know my good fortune. The volume seems to me to embody genuine research in the sense in which I understand the word, which differs from that advocated by many analysts. There are, I believe, three possible kinds of research. The first, which I do not favour, is a centrifugal one, in which psychoanalysis is extended beyond its boundaries and, on the honourable pretext of taking an interest in other disciplines, presented as the method of choice for obtaining knowledge of the unconscious. That is why some face-to-face therapies call themselves ‘psychoanalytic’ if that proves to their advantage. As to other disciplines,

such as the neurosciences or philosophy, it is hard to avoid claiming that they fail to take account of what the unconscious really is, on the grounds that their protagonists ‘have not had analysis’.

The second research approach, espoused to excellent effect by Kennedy, is centripetal, involving the selection from other disciplines or investigative fields (e.g. philosophy or sociology) of whatever is best able to help us grasp what we actually do as psychoanalysts. This kind of research, while less pretentious, is no less ambitious for that, based as it is on the experience of analytic treatment as offered to us by Freud, with its unique setting (the couch and the armchair)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

De la pratique analytique [On analytic practice]

Thierry Bokanowski. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France—Epitres. 1998. Pp. 172

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

15 Arden Road, London N3 3AB
williams@psyc.demon.co.uk

Paul Williams  

Sometimes, the genre of a book can evoke interest over and above its content, particularly if the book is from a culture other than one's own. Thierry Bokanowski's De la pratique analytique is one such example; a paperback from the well-known Paris publishing house PUF, which carries a title that has no precise equivalent in English. The difficulty with an English translation of the French title—‘On analytic practice’—is that it falls short of the multiple meanings intended in the French, inviting too much emphasis on practical issues such as physical surroundings, logistics etc. Perhaps the closest equivalent to ‘pratique’ is the Greek praxis—the personal articulation of a discipline or body of knowledge. Interestingly, the Old French meaning of ‘pratique’ is intercourse or correspondence (in the late sixteenth century a ‘pratique’ was a licence granted to a ship ‘to hold intercourse with a port, on showing a clean bill of health’).

Bokanowski tackles, if not the gamut of intercourse and correspondence between psychoanalyst and patient, then a considerable slice of it—an enterprise that would seem hardly conceivable in such a short text. However, his use of relaxed clear language to convey a range of analytic corcepts that are portrayed as conceptual and cultural ‘givens’ offers a clue to the fact that this work is idiosyncratic, whilst at the same time it is situated within an established theoretical and technical orientation of the French analytic tradition. It is a text written on behalf of a certain kind of readership from a particular cultural milieu. Having determined this much of the context, it is possible to read the book with interest and to acquire a useful perspective on French psychoanalysis, which is skilfully conveyed.

Bokanowski's book contains an introduction, five chapters and a conclusion. Chapter one is a historical overview of the origins and evolution of classical analytic technique, developments that were founded upon and inextricably tied to Freud's discovery of the transference relationship.

[[RunningHead]]

Adolescent Breakdown and Beyond

Moses Laufer. Madison, WI: International Universities Press. 1997. Pp. 156

Martin Silverman

Martin Silverman

551 Ridgewood Road
Maplewood, NJ 07040

Martin Silverman  

This monograph on the outpatient treatment of adolescents with serious emotional disturbance derives from the proceedings of a conference held at the Brent Adolescent Centre in London in October 1995. As the reader gradually becomes aware, the conference apparently was addressed to those in the community—school officials, primary-care physicians, clergymen, probation officers and so on—who are in a position to identify teenagers and young adults who are failing to weather the passage through the adolescent process and are sinking into serious emotional disturbance. They can direct them to help that is available to them if they recognise their need for such assistance.

The message conveyed by the book's contributors, who are past or present staff members of the Brent Centre, is two-fold. The first message is that signs of serious adolescent emotional disorder are too easily overlooked. Wishful thinking can lead to the erroneous assumption that left to their own devices the youngsters will ‘outgrow’ their problems. On the other hand, anxiety about the seriousness of the problems can propel an observer into denying dangers that urgently needed to be heeded. Concern for the discrepancy between the large number of youngsters in need of assistance and the limited resources available to meet their needs also can lead to such helplessness and paralysis that steps are not taken to offer assistance to at least some of them.

The second message is that courageous, competent, adequately trained professionals can provide an array of psychological services that can greatly assist even extremely troubled adolescents. In the pages of this slim volume, which is rich in clinical detail, we encounter young people who are crumbling in their adaptation to life's demands, withdrawing from relationships with other people, dropping out of school, defeating themselves masochistically, and/or tumbling into anorexia, binge-purging, self-mutilation, depression, despair and suicidal inclination. Some have lost parents in early childhood or at the height of their adolescent turmoil. Others have been subjected to the deleterious influence of alcoholic, depressed or paranoid parents. Still others have been emotionally cannibalised by parents who have not permitted them to break through the envelope of early parent—child unity so that they can hatch out into a separate existence of their own. These adolescents have hardly been prepared, enabled, or assisted in their efforts to rise to the opportunity to achieve the autonomy, self-direction and self-supervision afforded them by the advent of adolescence. They are not in a position to avail themselves in a healthy fashion of the chance to enter into the excitement and the satisfactions of mature sexual and emotional intimacy.

responseSet.title: Selected Writings by Anna Freud: Richard Ekins and Ruth Freeman. London and New York: Penguin Books. 1998. Pp. 304
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Selected Writings by Anna Freud

Richard Ekins and Ruth Freeman. London and New York: Penguin Books. 1998. Pp. 304

Arthur Couch

Arthur Couch

79 Redington Road, London NW3 7RR

Arthur Couch  

The editors' deep knowledge of Anna Freud's thinking has resulted in an astute selection of papers from 1936 to 1981 that covers the full range of her seminal contributions to psychoanalysis. It brings together in one book a collection of key papers that originally appeared in her first book and in many journals over the years, which makes it extremely convenient to the interested analyst, reader, teacher or student who wants a condensed but highly representative review of Anna Freud's works. A careful reading of these papers should reconfirm Anna Freud's position as one of the most brilliant minds in psychoanalysis this century and as a diamondclear writer who can make the most complex topics seem simple and straightforward.

Although perhaps not intended as such, these selected writings, if read in temporal sequence, give a most incisive summary of the rise and fall of classical Freudian psychoanalysis from Sigmund Freud's time to the period of decline in the mid-1970s when the pluralistic trends increasingly decentred psychoanalysis. Anna Freud's major writings from the time of her book The Ego and the Mechanisms of Defence (1936) through the 1960s were her original contributions to the growing mainstream of Freudian adult and child psychoanalysis. It is interesting to re-enter in her writings this period of the high tide of Freudian psychoanalysis in which there was such confidence and enthusiasm. Then from the 1970s Anna Freud tried to contest the validity of the alternative theories and techniques of the expanding English Schools of analysis that had earlier been quite minor influences on the international scene.

1044

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Adolescent Development, Psychopathology, and Treatment

H. Spencer Bloch. Madison, CT: Int. Univ. Press. Inc. 1995. Pp. viii + 417

Steven Wein

Steven Wein

125 East 87th Street, New York, NY 10128

Steven Wein  

One of the prevailing trends in psychoanalysis is to look to developmentally earlier modes of experience to understand later psychopathology and character formation. It is therefore a pleasure to read an important contribution to psychoanalysis that reviews and expands our understanding of adolescence. It is during adolescence that the major issues of development may be resolved, and most adult psychopathology can be understood in terms of the various outcomes of adolescent struggles. A readable, sophisticated and clinically based textbook of adolescent psychoanalysis is therefore a welcome addition to the psychoanalytic literature.

H. Spencer Bloch, a child and adolescent analyst associated with the San Francisco Institute, explains that his text was intended to be

an easy-reading, clinically oriented text that enunciated the long-standing view of adolescent development, discussed the challenges to that theory, summarised relevant contributions from other fields, and included references for those who wished to explore the latter in depth … to include … and reconcile those of the older and newer findings that I have found serviceable into a view of the developmental process (vii).

His book succeeds in these goals.

Bloch outlines his central thesis in the first chapter.

1051

responseSet.title: Julia Kristeva: Speaking the Unspeakable: Anne-Marie Smith. London: Pluto Press. 1998. Pp. 110
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Julia Kristeva: Speaking the Unspeakable

Anne-Marie Smith. London: Pluto Press. 1998. Pp. 110

Giuseppina Antinucci-Mark

Giuseppina Antinucci-Mark

14 Montenotte Road, London N8 8RL

Giuseppina Antinucci-Mark  

In Julia Kristeva: Speaking the Unspeakable, Anne-Marie Smith, who teaches literary translation at the Université de Paris VIII, gives a critical introduction to Kristeva's oeuvre. She proposes two clear and definite hermeneutic keys, consisting in a close reading and translation of the texts and a reflection on psychoanalytic theory as essential for comprehending and evaluating Kristeva's work.

The author does not purport to give the reader an exhaustive account of Kristeva's work over thirty years, but rather to address the issue of the fortune of her thought within the Anglo-American world. She depicts the impact of the Kristevan texts upon the English-speaking readership as a mixture of fascination, seduction and estrangement, rooted in profound and irreducible cultural differences between France, Britain and America.

This divergence is epitomised in the difference between the French féminin, meaning ‘that which belongs to women’ and the English feminine, which connotes culturally imposed gender attributes. Kristeva's preoccupation is with the representation of the féminin, as the ‘maternal continent’ and the Anglo-American feminists attack her for not being separatist enough, ultimately showing a profound misunderstanding of the continuity of her thinking within the tradition of Freudian psychoanalysis.

This is another significant difference between the two cultural traditions. In France Freudian thought is very alive indeed, re-examined and reelaborated by not only the psychoanalytic community, but the intellectual world at large, whereas beyond the Channel Freud is customarily attacked and vilified. Thus a closer reading of Speaking the Unspeakable reveals how far Smith poses the problem of the text, its translation, its ‘translatability’ but also its limits.

Smith examines Kristeva's most recent writings, pouvoirs de l'horreur, Étrangers à nousmêmes, Histoires d'amour, Soleil noir, Les nouvelles maladies de l'âme, Les temps sensibles, Sens et non-sens de la révolte, reaching back to her doctoral thesis, La révolution du langage poétique, to find continuity and coherence as well as transformation in the theme of the inscription of archaic, pre-verbal experience, the ‘semantic’ within ‘symbolic’ language.

responseSet.title: Épître aux Oedipiens [Epistle to the Oedipans]: Jean Cournut. Paris: Presses Univ. France, Collection Épîtres. 1998. Pp. 230
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Épître aux Oedipiens [Epistle to the Oedipans]

Jean Cournut. Paris: Presses Univ. France, Collection Épîtres. 1998. Pp. 230

Thierry Bokanowski

Thierry Bokanowski

48, rue des Francs-Bourgeois, 75003 Paris

Thierry Bokanowski  

Jean Cournut reminds us by his title that every human being is by nature bound to be an ‘Oedipan’. After many years of studying the effects of the drives and of sexuality on ‘psychic work’ (1991, L'ordinaire de la passion. Paris: Presses Univ. France, Fil rouge; 1993, M. Cournut-Janin & J. Cournut, La castration et le féminin dans les deux sexes. Rev. franç. Psychanal., 52, Special Congress issue: 1333–1558; 1996, J. Cournut et al., Psychanalyse et sexualité. Questions aux sciences humaines. Paris: Dunod, Collection Inconscient et Culture), the author here turns to one of the essential questions of present-day psychoanalysis: what is the place of the Oedipus complex for the psychoanalyst today as he listens to and theorises on the basis of his patients’ material?

In his original, pungent and humorous style, Cournut offers the reader a perspective view and a reassessment of the attention currently vouchsafed by the entire psychoanalytic community, in its clinical and theoretical debates, to (1) the Oedipus complex and its corollary, the castration complex, both of which take account of the organisation of the drives; (2) their end-points and limits, which may have to do with the ‘passions’, the organisation of ‘character’ and other factors; and (3) sexuality, which is too often concealed or even evacuated in contemporary psychoanalytic thought.

There are a number of reasons, both inside and outside psychoanalysis, why the author considers that the Oedipus complex, as the bedrock of Freud's ‘discovery’, now being not so much rejected as trivialised, needs to have its former status restored.

Firstly, outside the analytic field, the Oedipus complex is coming to be seen as a fact of ‘culture’, which can consequently be filed away in the archives of acquired knowledge as relevant more to the history of epistemology than to an enquiry into psychic functioning and its vicissitudes. Secondly, within psychoanalysis itself, we are liable to find ourselves shunning sexuality and ‘everything that flows from the difference between the sexes and the generations’ if we follow certain psychoanalytic schools in focusing solely on what precedes the phase of oedipal organisation around the castration complex, i.e. on such aspects as the early relationship, the archaic and the primal, narcissism and intersubjectivity, the preverbal and the pregenital.

responseSet.title: The 41st International Psychoanalytic Congress, Santiago, Chile 1999: Foreword
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The 41st International Psychoanalytic Congress, Santiago, Chile 1999

Foreword

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro

Payne Whitney Clinic, Box 147
525 East 68th Street
New York
NY 10021

Theodore Shapiro  

Many have noted that Freud offered no definitive theory of affects (Rapaport, 1953). Nonetheless, Freud's psychoanalysis is affect-centred, focusing on anxiety as the mediating force that intervenes between drive and defence. He also wrote cogently regarding the affect of melancholia (1917), which he analogised to mourning. There is a third theme in Freud's (1895) early writing, in which he invoked Darwin as the source of an idea that hysteria and anxiety, among other affects, are remnants of earlier experiences of humankind that remain a part of our experiential legacy as affective expressions.

These are the explicit Freudian roots in our psychoanalytic history that warrant attention regarding the theory of affects. There is additional implicit evidence that psychoanalysis has,par-excellence, been a science of the mind that considers affect as a central working concept and does not split cognition from affect because human experience is viewed holistically. In fact, in psychoanalytic writing there is no clinical description of thought without affect. We see the intermingling of both reflected in self-evaluative responses, the colouration of retold experience or any recall of human interaction. We measure each experience against affect-laden past reminiscences or transferential constellations that are suffused with feeling. Our case histories are construed and weighted by their affective valence. We become witness to change within the psychoanalytic context that involves the careful titration of feeling. We speak of taming affects, or the possibility of making experiences with high affective tone less poignant in the service of adaptation.

The participants of the 41st Congress of the IPA will explore these themes and others: our three plenaries will focus on the development of affect, affect and language, and affect as it influences technique. This programmatic breakdown is, perforce, artificial in so far as every clinical event or for that matter every human interaction is accompanied by affect. Nonetheless, these plenaries and the complementary panels will try to tease apart the functional role of affective experience in the broad array of psychoanalytically oriented activities, including clinical, extra-clinical, and applied domains of enquiry.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Les bébés de l'inconscient. Le psychanalyste face aux stérilités féminines aujourd'hui [The babies of the unconscious. The psychoanalyst and female sterility today]

Sylvie Faure-Pragier. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France, Collection Le fait psychanalytique. 1997. Pp. 256

Annick Le Guen

Annick Le Guen

2, Place de Séoul, 75014 Paris

Annick Le Guen  

By sharing with us the questions and hypotheses that have arisen during the course of her lengthy experience as a psychoanalyst in private practice and in a hospital unit for medically assisted pregnancies (Service du Pr. Mauvais-Jarvis, at the Necker Hospital in Paris), the author invites us to join her in re-examining sterility and the psychic functioning of sterile women. Her work as a consulting psychoanalyst and therapist has thus led her to pursue the theory of womanhood and femininity to greater depth, to modify the definition of the various forms of sterility, and to review the conditions for their treatment. Since the request for a child does not necessarily correspond to the wish for one, it is found that the ‘cure’ of sterility is not necessarily simply a matter of ‘having a child’, but sometimes involves acknowledging and consciously accepting the rejection of motherhood. This book would be important even if that were all the author had to say.

Assisted-pregnancy techniques meet with seemingly inexplicable ‘resistances of the body’, which thwart any possibility of fertilisation or of carrying a pregnancy to full term. This suggests that a baby is made not only with the body but also with the psyche, especially as the latter—as Sylvie Faure-Pragier shows—also plays an ‘active’ part in preventing a successful pregnancy in cases of sterility with proven medical causes, which ought therefore in principle to be reversible by medical methods alone. She rightly concludes that psychic involvement is in no way precluded in such circumstances: such women have the same foundations of psychic functioning as others and react to the diagnosis of sterility with the same conscious and unconscious feelings as their counterparts deemed to be suffering from ‘psychogenic sterility’.

‘Psychic’ involvement in sterility is always bound to aggravate or give rise to other (physical) dysfunctions, which have their own effects and fuel conflicts. This vicious circle, in which the relative roles of the causes and consequences of sterility become difficult to distinguish, is described by the author in terms of the recursiveness of sterility, from the mind to the body and the body to the mind, in which it is not always possible to say precisely which came first.

responseSet.title: ‘Psychoanalysis and Cognitive-Evolutionary Psychology: An Attempt at Integration’ by Paolo Migone and Giovanni Liotti
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Psychoanalysis and Cognitive-Evolutionary Psychology

An Attempt at Integration’ by Paolo Migone and Giovanni Liotti

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

15 Arden Road, London N3 3AB
williams@psyc.demon.co.uk

Paul Williams  

This paper appeared in the journal (Int. J. Psychoanal., 1998, 79: 1071) and was placed on the journal's web-site several weeks in advance of publication. In the paper the authors argue that the abandonment of the theory of trauma in 1897 was a trauma for Freud, who reacted with an over-emphasis on inner fantasies and drive discharge. Today we witness the ‘abandonment of drive theory’, i.e. the notion that human beings strive not primarily to reduce sexual and aggressive drives but rather seek objects, assign meanings, test previous beliefs and assimilate new schemes. The authors argue that the clinical and theoretical insights of psychoanalysis must join with the now available and converging lines of evidence coming from cognitive psychology, neurobiology, evolutionary epistemology, ethology, infant research and psychotherapy research. Although there are dangers in combining disciplines, psychoanalysis cannot postpone its confrontation with other scientific disciplines. Migone & Liotti's paper is a complex one (as is the discussion), and this review is a brief summary only of certain main points.

The authors offer a theory of therapy and mental functioning using language that will be intelligible to various disciplines, from cognitive psychology, to neurobiology and to psychoanalysis. The proposed model involves the reformulation of the psychoanalytic theory of motivation and a revision of the conception of inherited information. They discuss ‘Control-Mastery Theory’ proposed by the San Francisco Psychotherapy Research Group to suggest a conceptualisation of clinical theory in the light of cognitive science, and they cite Edelman's evolutionary neurobiology and ethology.

Freud believed that people inherit residues of the experiences of previous generations, according to Lamarck's ideas. He eventually built a metapsychology in which he came to conceptualise a dual theory of drives (life and death, or Eros and aggression). Corrections to the concept of drives were made by ego-psychology (Hartmann, 1939) and by object-relations theory. Different autonomous motivational forces were emphasised: object-seeking, attachment, contact comfort, exploration, curiosity, competence-mastery, and so on. At the same time Sullivan and the other neo-Freudians or ‘culturalists’ of the Washington School of Psychiatry criticised libido theory and emphasised the importance of ‘real’ interpersonal experiences for the construction of personality. In recent years, infant research and motivation studies provided evidence that the child is regulated by many discrete motivational systems concerned with interpersonal adaptation.

Facing It Out. Clinical Perspectives on Adolescent Disturbance

Robin Anderson and Anna Dartington. London: Duckworth. Tavistock Clinic Series, edited by Nicholas Temple and Margot Waddell. 1998. Pp. 177

A. Brafman

A. Brafman

20 Hollycroft Avenue, London NW3 7QL

A. Brafman  

Nicholas Temple and Margot Waddell have edited a valuable series of books describing the work of the present departments of the Tavistock Clinic. Facing It Out, edited by Robin Anderson and Anna Dartington, presents ‘clinical perspectives on adolescent disturbance’ and it gives a rich and instructive portrait of the work of the Adolescent Department of the Tavistock Clinic. True to the words of the editors of the series, this book is evidence of the clinic's tradition of developing ‘a wide range of psychotherapeutic approaches to mental health that have from the beginning been strongly influenced by psychoanalysis’ (p. vii).

Clinical descriptions are detailed and clear.

841

responseSet.title: The Process of Remembering: Recovery and Discovery
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Process of Remembering

Recovery and Discovery

Ivan Fónagy

Ivan Fónagy

1 Sq. Claude Debussy
92169 Antony
France

Ivan Fónagy  

Experience of gradually recovering lost memories may shed some light on the cognitive mechanism underlying remembering. We (1) easily remember the external frame (the context) of the lost memory; (2) experience the emergence of its internal frame (category or genre); (3) recall its configuration, its rhythmic skeleton or its dynamic structure; (4) and even sketch it by a gesture; (5) recall our evaluation of the person or our impression of the event we cannot remember; (6) find the central object may emerge in a disguised (symbolic) form; (7) find the abortive first attempt to reconstruct the lost memory may contain an unconscious interpretation of the hidden event or the forgotten dream. Gradual remembering follows on the whole the path of verbal evolution. Trying to recapture lost memories we are compelled to make use of preverbal forms of mental elaboration and expression (visual thinking, gesture language, symbols). At the same time, recovery of lost memories has much in common with the procedure of scientific discovery. Discovery could be considered as a paradoxical form of remembering: recovering the unknown. Scientific metaphors uncover (‘remember’) preconscious and unconscious knowledge. In his studies on Farkas Bólyai, Imre Hermann made an attempt to interpret scientific theories much in the same way that Freud, Jones, Rank, Reik, Hanns Sachs, Róheim analysed myths, rituals, literary and artistic works. He traces back some essential features of Bólyai's discovery to repressed early memories and fantasies of the great mathematician.
