responseSet.title: Transfert contre-transfert [Transference/Countertransference]: Jean Guillaumin. Paris: Éditions L'Esprit du Temps. 1998. Pp. 266
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transfert contre-transfert [Transference/Countertransference]

Jean Guillaumin. Paris: Éditions L'Esprit du Temps. 1998. Pp. 266

C. Atlan

C. Atlan

33 rue Censier, 75005 Paris

C. Atlan  

Psychoanalysis today has a predilection for reevaluating its major concepts. So it is that intersubjectivists, protagonists of object-relations theory and classical psychoanalysts have entered the lists on the transference and the countertransference. Jean Guillaumin's fifth book is devoted to this topic, which is so important in French psychoanalysis. The work is the culmination of an abundant output of books, papers and chapters of multi-author publications written between 1972 and 1998. The subjects discussed are dreams, trauma, psychoanalytic technique, the object, the transference and the ‘negative’. The author thus covers the entire range of major psychoanalytic issues confronting today's practitioner.

Guillaumin takes us to the very heart of our daily practice in a work whose title, ‘Transference/Countertransference’, brings together the two aspects of one and the same reality, which the author describes as follows:

two psychic apparatuses intertwined in the work of analysis, an absolutely new working dyad that cannot be reduced to a mere repetition irrevocably lived in the present, even if the characteristic feature of that repetition is that it draws us towards the timeless through regression to the unconscious … it is an intimate sharing, an asymmetric simultaneity, tantamount to the laying down of a new origin à deux that will always leave an unanalysable residue (p. 204).

Although the author stresses the inseparability of the transference and the countertransference, it is the latter that interests him most—the domain, he tells us, that Freud left relatively unexplored and bequeathed to us together with the responsibility for filling in the gaps.

Rather than presenting a linear account, I shall attempt a thematic review of the book, which is based on the second topography and brings together all the major themes of the period after 1920—the negative therapeutic reaction, the death drive, personal and group identifications and, finally, splitting—in a structural conception of the psychic apparatus.

[[RunningHead]]

Amor—Enamoramiento—Pasión [Love—falling in love—passion]

María Cristina Melgar. Buenos Aires: Kargieman. 1997. Pp. 172

Norah Galimany

Norah Galimany

Fray J. Sta. Ma. de Oro 2789—1° ‘B’
1425 Capital Federal—Argentina

Norah Galimany  

The elegant presentation of this volume provides a foretaste of the treatment the author gives to her subject. The very first encounter with the book reveals an integration of form and content whereby aesthetic impact is used as the approach of choice to ‘becoming acquainted’ with the themes to be discussed. There is an aesthetic side, too, to Melgar's understanding of the hypotheses of psychoanalysis, which is complemented by her writing style. Let us not forget the beauty of

Freud's writing, which is in my view a pendant to his discoveries—not to mention the inherent beauty of the discoveries themselves. This almost bodily register impels us to use different strategies and investigative methods in attempting to capture in words something of the affective vibration expressed. Freud himself gives us a masterly example in his attunement to the musicality—and this in a man who claimed to hate music!

1262

[[RunningHead]]

I. La rencontre analytique [The first analytic meeting]. Revue française de psychanalyse, II. Le masculin [Maleness and the male]. Revue française de psychanalyse

62 (1): 1-385, 1998. 62 (2): 386-702, 1998

Bérengère De Senarclens

Bérengère De Senarclens

16, Plateau de Champel, 1206 Geneva

Bérengère De Senarclens  

I. La Rencontre Analytique

The first 1998 issue of Revue française de psychanalyse is devoted to the theoretical and clinical problems posed by the patient's ‘first meeting’ with the psychoanalyst. It contains interesting reflections on the offer of an analytic setting, the foundations of commitment to a treatment and the adaptation of analytic practice to present-day changes in pathology. As we ponder the future of psychoanalysis at the end of the present century, this volume expresses the concern to ‘keep the psychoanalytic ship firmly on course’ (p. 90). The various contributions display confidence in the ability of the Freudian and post-Freudian edifice to imagine technical and therapeutic modifications based on socio-cultural trends. The difficulty, however, is to steer a satisfactory course between the Scylla of a suffocating contraction of the field of application of analytic practice and the Charybdis of a drift towards techniques remote from analysis of the transference.

The first analytic meeting could perhaps be defined as what René Diatkine has called an ‘attempt to prevent the psychic apparatus from going round in circles’ (p. 135). The papers presented in this issue can certainly be seen in this light. Analysts here allow us to share in the specificity of their experience in applications of psychoanalysis as diverse as psychosomatic medicine and work with adolescents, babies, psychotics and even prisoners. These practitioners reflect on the ‘outlines of their activity’ and the technical variations they are able to contemplate. They seek to show how the inevitably destabilising singularity of the meeting with the psychoanalyst should be capable of representing a moment of ‘passage’ and of ‘constituting an inaugural phase of the establishment of a new form of psychic functioning’ (p. 5).

184

responseSet.title: Unformulated Experience: From Dissociation to Imagination in Psychoanalysis: Donnel B. Stern. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 1997. Pp. 255
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Unformulated Experience: From Dissociation to Imagination in Psychoanalysis

Donnel B. Stern. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 1997. Pp. 255

Judy Kantrowitz

Judy Kantrowitz

334 Kent St., Brookline, MA 02446

Judy Kantrowitz  

Reflection is at the centre of the psychoanalytic experience. In his book, Unformulated Experience, Donnel Stern specifies and defines the conditions necessary for creative reflection. It is his contention that only by finding words that grasp pre-reflective, unformulated experiences is it possible to have the perspective to see and think about one's life meaningfully. Once unconscious experience is consciously reflected upon, it is shaped not only by the past but also by the language by which it is described. The book is an ambitious work that brings together hermeneutic theory, most specifically the work of Gadamer, and interpersonal and relational psychoanalysis, particularly a contemporary reading of the work of Harry Stack Sullivan. Stern's view is that all understanding emerges from an interaction, shaped by the individual qualities of both participants and acquiring unique properties from their engagement with each other. From this interpersonal field, both imaginative understanding and dissociation of unformulated experiences may result. Which of these outcomes occurs depends on the nature of the interpersonal engagement at any particular moment.

A central proposition of his book is that dissociation keeps unformulated experiences from ever becoming conscious; in contrast, repression extrudes ‘fully formed unconscious content’ from consciousness. Through dissociation the interpretation of experience is avoided; no concept and therefore no language is applied to the experience. These experiences lack the clarity and differentiation for the usual defensive operations to be applied; they are not formed enough to distort or forget. In work with traumatised patients, these are familiar ideas. However, Stern has introduced the concepts in relation to psychoanalytic work in a manner that affords a greater appreciation of their application to all patients and all people.

In the first section of the book, Stern lays out the paradox of experience being both pre-existing, which is to be discovered, and newly formed, which is currently created. He places these concepts in the context of present-day psychoanalytic theory of social constructivism, experience as co-created. Understanding experience, he maintains, requires symbolic representation, which language provides. Language is the condition for experiencing the interpretation of the experience itself. The conscious aim of psychoanalysts is to find meaning in experience by finding words to comprehend non-verbal experience. Through the course of making these experiences explicit, a new creation may occur which may change the experiences themselves. Although his own use of language tends to be turgid, and there is a repetitiveness that becomes burdensome, it is worth persevering. The ideas he proposes are engaging, and his positions are clearly stated.

responseSet.title: ‘Getting in on the act: the hysterical solution’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Getting in on the act: the hysterical solution’

Allen Esterson

Allen Esterson

1A Cromwell Grove
London W6 7RQ
allenesterson@compuserve.com

Allen Esterson  

Dear Sir,

According to the abstract, in his article ‘Getting in on the act: the hysterical solution’ (1999) Ronald Britton incorporates ‘additional information now available to us about Breuer's treatment of Bertha Pappenheim’, most notably that bearing on ‘the erotic transference … culminating in her hysterical pregnancy and labour’ (p. 1). In the article itself this event is treated as ‘knowledge’ that Freud possessed about Pappenheim, and hence legitimate material to incorporate into Britton's analysis of the case of Anna O. Now although Freud never published this material, in ‘On the history of the psychoanalytic movement’ he alludes to an ‘untoward event’ which led Breuer to break off ‘all further investigation’ of Pappenheim (1914, p. 12), and writes that Breuer ‘never said this to me in so many words, but he told me enough at different times to justify this reconstruction of what happened’ (my italics). There is a similar allusion to the termination of the treatment in An Autobiographical Study (1925, pp. 26-7), where Freud writes about how he ‘came to interpret the case correctly and to reconstruct, from some remarks which [Breuer] had made, the conclusion of his treatment of it’ (my italics).

The fact that on each occasion Freud states he reconstructed the ‘untoward event’ suggests that one should be cautious about assuming the authenticity of the phantom pregnancy. But what do we find in Britton's account?

However, we now know that on one hot summer evening of 1883 and whilst [Freud] and Breuer dined alone together in relaxed mood he was told another much more unbuttoned, informal and intimate account of the case. This revealed the erotic psycho-drama that

took place within Breuer's treatment and potentially gave Freud raw material for his theories of the Oedipus complex, identification, transference, countertransference, repetition compulsion and acting out (pp. 1-2).

How do we ‘now know’ that this meeting ‘revealed the erotic psycho-drama’ culminating (by implication) in the phantom pregnancy? Britton does not tell us, but a little research reveals the most likely sources of this beguiling vignette. The story of the phantom pregnancy was published by Ernest Jones, who writes that ‘confirmation of this account can be found in a contemporary letter Freud wrote to Martha [Bernays] which contains substantially the same story’ (Jones, 1953, p. 247). Now although access to Freud's correspondence to Martha is restricted, a translation of the relevant passage in the letter in question (31 October 1883) is given in John Forrester's essay ‘The true story of Anna O.’ in his book The Seductions of Psychoanalysis; but there is no trace of the phantom pregnancy, only a report of Breuer's concern at his wife's depression which he correctly identified as arising from her jealousy ‘of the demands made on her husband by another woman’ (Forrester, 1990, p. 19). So whence comes the account of the cosy tête-à-têite between Freud and Breuer at which all was revealed? I can only presume it relates to another letter published in partial form in Forrester's book (and in the volume of letters edited by Ernst Freud), that of 13 July 1883.

responseSet.title: Response
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Ronald Britton

Ronald Britton

24 Hillfield Road
London NW6 1PZ

Ronald Britton  

Dear Sir,

You have asked me to respond to the letter from Esterson and I will do so as briefly as I can. Before taking issue in any detail with the points he makes I would like to make clear the purpose of my paper. It was originally part of a symposium intended to review the place of sexuality in psychoanalytic theory today, a hundred years on from the Studies on Hysteria. I chose to write a paper on a theory of my own on hysteria and to include in it a review of the case of ‘Anna O.’ in the light of current psychoanalytic thinking and the information available to us since Freud's death and Jones's revelation of her identity in 1953. I was already familiar with this material and had been stimulated by visiting an apartment she occupied in Frankfurt and the little museum in Neu-Isenburg where she founded ‘Das Heim des Jüdischen Frauenbundes’.

None of Esterson's quotations from Freud is unfamiliar to me and the sources I have used for the story of Bertha Pappenheim's erotic transference, regression and ‘false labour’ are those he alludes to in his letter. The difference that lies between us is in our reading of Freud's public texts, our trust in Freud's veracity in his private communications, and our attitudes to psychoanalytic enquiry.

1239

responseSet.title: The Pre-Oedipal Father-Son Relationship in Korean Myths and in the Patient Today
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Pre-Oedipal Father-Son Relationship in Korean Myths and in the Patient Today

Heikyöng Moser-Ha

Heikyöng Moser-Ha

Othmarstr. 8, 8008 Zürich

Heikyöng Moser-Ha  

The author uses the material of Korean myths and folktale and from a patient to understand the way in which the Oedipus complex is negotiated and resolved in Korean society. In Korean myth and folktale a benign and supportive pre-oedipal father—son relationship is more appreciated and encouraged than the competition and rivalry in the oedipal father—son relationship. Consequently, in Korean myth the boy can develop balanced masculinity through the father's encouragement and protection and set out on his own journey to be a man as strong as his father. Oedipus, in contrast, was abandoned by the parent, which led him to act out his unconscious desire to kill the father, ending with total destruction. The difficulties of applying western-based psychoanalytic theory to Korean culture are discussed. Although we are influenced by historical, social and cultural patterns of external reality, we are more influenced by our unconscious than we are aware of, which enables us to share current psychoanalytic knowledge to understand Korean myths and patients without much deviation.

responseSet.title: Renik replies to Cavell
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Renik replies to Cavell

Owen Renik

Owen Renik

244 Myrtle St.
San Francisco
CA 94109

Owen Renik  

Dear Sir,

I'm delighted to have the opportunity to briefly respond to Marcia Cavell's critique (int. J. Psychoanal., 79: 1195–1202) of my article, ‘The analyst's subjectivity and the analyst's objectivity’ (Int. J. Psychoanal., 79: 487-497). I think we should begin with the report of her own clinical work that Marcia offers us, because it illustrates what happens when her point of view about truth, reality and the analyst's position in relation to them holds sway in the treatment situation. Marcia writes: ‘A patient of mine had suddenly begun to annoy me quite a lot. I was worried about the intensity of my annoyance. No question that the patient was, though unconsciously, out to annoy me…’ (p. 1198).

No question? I would say, rather, that there's always a question when one person judges unconscious motivation on the part of another. Marcia has transformed her inference, with all its personal determinants, into an indisputable fact of observation.

382

Response

Mary Target

Mary Target

Gower St
London WCIE 6BT

Mary Target  

Dear Sir,

Thank you for giving me the opportunity to respond to Dr Chefetz's letter about my book review essay on the Recovered Memory Controversy. I would also like to thank Dr Chefetz for raising criticisms that would be, I am sure, shared by many psychoanalysts. I respect these views and the clinical experience which informs them, and I hope it will become clear that the contrast between our views is less sharp than he assumes. However, while accusing me of bias and distortion, Dr Chefetz used rhetoric and caricature of my views (including views I did not express), sometimes in preference to reasoned debate. Anyone who might doubt the accuracy of a patient's newly recovered memories is charged with showing a non-analytic stance. Yet one key to Freud's great discoveries is the close scrutiny of seemingly obvious facts, the search for complex explanations where the mind (and perhaps the patient) would have us accept simple answers. The approach shown in Dr Chefetz's letter polarises and personalises the discussion in a way that almost always seems to happen in this particular controversy, and that seems to me worthy of considerable reflection. I will consider the important points he made about technique and the clinical situation, after responding—within the space available —to his comments on my understanding of the material reviewed.

Dr Chefetz first seeks to illustrate the sloppy scholarship of some parts of the essay (sometimes ‘grossly misleading and/or unsupported by the data’), saying that ‘This statement is a case in point: “On the analyst's side, why would he or she look for, or accept as accurate, the reporting of previously forgotten sexual abuse, given the evidence that such recovered memories are very likely to be mistaken?” (p. 1025)’. I am not clear why the offending ‘statement’ (in fact, deliberately, a question, aiming to explore doubts such as those he raises) is a case of misleading and unsupported assertions. It might have been the reference to evidence that recovered memories of previously forgotten sexual abuse are likely to be mistaken, which had been summarised earlier in the essay. Alternatively, Dr Chefetz may have objected to my speculations as to what pressures might lead an analyst to seek explanations in terms of early abuse, or to endorse them as factual when he or she is not in a position to know this.

Giovanna Goretti

Via Panama 114,00198 Rome

Giovanna Goretti  

According to Speziale Bagliacca—a training analyst at the Italian Psychoanalytical Society and author of On Freud's Shoulders and other works—helping the patient to manage the sense of guilt is a sine qua non of every psychoanalytic treatment intended to cure. Yes, cure. In the face of all the cultural and pseudo-cultural fashions that assign different aims to psychoanalysis, Speziale Bagliacca has the courage to use the word ‘cure’, which he defines as a

clear and stable diminution of suffering, accompanied by an increased capacity to contain it, with all the relevant implications in terms of disappearance of symptoms, control of the compulsion to act in accordance with rigid, fixed patterns, as well as the capacity to think (p. 32).

393

responseSet.title: Lesbian Lives: Psychoanalytic Narratives Old and New: Maggie Magee and Diana C. Miller. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1997. Pp. 407
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lesbian Lives: Psychoanalytic Narratives Old and New

Maggie Magee and Diana C. Miller. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1997. Pp. 407

Susan Vaughan

Susan Vaughan

722 West 168th Street, New York, NY 10032

Susan Vaughan  

In their brief introduction to this book, which represents a major step forward in psychoanalytic thinking about the lives of lesbian women, the authors ask the reader to imagine three standing bookcases:

The first bookcase contains works by psychoanalysts; the second contains works by feminists; the third contains the work of gay and lesbian writers. A few texts can be shelved in either the first or second bookcase because their authors identify as psychoanalysts and as feminists. Some works can be placed in either the second or third cases because their authors identify as feminists and as lesbian. When we began our own writing, not one book could be filed on either the first or third bookcase as the work of a homosexually identified psychoanalyst (p. xxix).

Now Lesbian Lives could occupy any of the three shelves, and that fact alone is enough to make Magee and Miller's work important. But their contribution goes far beyond that, incorporating and synthesising an impressive range of psychoanalytic theories, biological studies, and clinical case material that is seasoned with lesbian poetry, literary criticism and gender studies.

The book begins with the authors confessing that when they began psychoanalytic training, they believed that they would come to understand the aetiology of female homosexuality and wanted to move towards a psychoanalytic understanding of lesbian identity that neither pathologised lesbians nor accepted lesbianism as something ‘pre-given’. But they rapidly began to see ‘that the apparently simple question “What causes female homosexuality?” contained unquestioned assumptions about the feelings, behaviors and relationships of women who love women’ (p. xxiii). These unquestioned assumptions have been combined with the nearly twenty centuries of historical omissions and silences that the authors review, including silence on the part of psychoanalytic institutes. By the end of the introduction we learn that the focus of the authors has shifted. Rather than trying to pinpoint one or several or even many developmental pathways to lesbianism and rather than trying to answer questions of aetiology, the authors focus instead on the developmental and psychological consequences of identifying as homosexual and of having lesbian relationships.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and the Neurosciences: A Topical Debate on Dreams1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and the Neurosciences

A Topical Debate on Dreams1

Mauro Mancia

Mauro Mancia

Via S. Marta, 13
20123 Milan

Mauro Mancia  

The author begins by pointing out that, whereas Freud first turned his attention to dreams in 1895, they became an object of neuroscientific interest only in the 1950s, after the discovery of rapid-eye-movement (REM) sleep and the observation that a subject woken in an REM phase could remember and narrate them. He discusses the various brain structures found by the neuroscientists to be implicated in dreaming and the associated hypotheses about their involvement in the processes of remembering dreams, their spatial construction and semantic organisation, and the dreamer's emotional participation in and narration of dreams. Attention is drawn to recent psychophysiological research findings indicating that dreaming occurs in all sleep phases and not only in REM episodes. The cognitivist contribution is also discussed. The author goes on to demonstrate the difference between the neuroscientific and psychoanalytic approaches to dreams. Whereas the neuroscientists are interested in the structures involved in dream production and in dream organisation and narratability, psychoanalysis concentrates on the meaning of dreams and on placing them in the context of the analytic relationship in accordance with the affective history of the dreamer and the transference. The brain structures and functions of interest to the neurosciences, while constituting the physical and biological substrate of these aspects, are stated to be irrelevant to their psychoanalytic understanding.

responseSet.title: Linking Lost Voices: Reading ‘The Dead’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Linking Lost Voices

Reading ‘The Dead’

Alice Jones

Alice Jones

CA 94618

Alice Jones  

The author presents an individual reading of James Joyce's short story, ‘The Dead’ from ‘Dubliners’. Language evokes subjective responses and associations that become part of the depth of experience one finds in a book. The author describes how uses of language give the reader a sense of sharing the main character's experience. She focuses on nodal moments in the story, in which external and internal worlds intersect. Images focused on include those of seeing and touching, inside and outside, crossing gaps between worlds and the way in which these sensory events shift the character's knowledge of his internal objects. The metaphor of lost voice highlights the presence of the dead throughout the story. The main character's evolution from being in a constricted state to one of expanded self-awareness is discussed. Britton's concept of triangular space is used to describe the character's transformation at the story's conclusion.

responseSet.title: Chinatown
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Chinatown

Noel Hess

Noel Hess

4th Floor, Cecil Fleming House
Grafton Way
London WC1E 6AU

Noel Hess  

Director: Roman Polanski

Distributor: Paramount

As is often true for the patient's first words in a session, the opening image of Chinatown (1974) sets the scene for the particular psychological terrain that the film will explore. The first thing we see is a photograph of a sexual couple. Clearly, the couple, caught in an act of private passion, have been seen by a hidden third party, the photographer. A series of photographs depicts the intercourse. This is accompanied by a moaning sound from the person (as yet unseen) who is looking at, or being shown, these photographs. There is a momentary ambiguity—is it a moan of voyeuristic excitement or of jealous agony? Quite quickly, the conventions and context of the scene are established. The photographs are being shown by a ‘private eye’ to the husband of the woman photographed. The husband is in a rage, and says, in almost the first words we hear: ‘She's just no good’, and soon after, ‘I think I'll kill her’.

While the initial ambiguities resolve into a conventional situation, one we feel familiar with within the genre, it is important to hold on to that initial strangeness, because it does throw us into the situation of the primal scene, of oedipal rage at the sexual mother and the private eye that watches. Chinatown will explore this territory of incestuous longings, and subtly contrast it with actual incest. In an important contribution Simon (1992) has shown how, in what he describes as ‘the history of an error’, these two radically different but related situations are often muddled, both in

our thinking and in the history of psychoanalysis. He compares incest survivors with Holocaust survivors, and by almost equating incest, or sexual abuse (and it is interesting to note how the latter term has now replaced the former) with oedipal urges, psychoanalysis has done a serious disservice to the real and devastating trauma of incest. The main character of Chinatown, the private eye, J. J. Gittes, makes a similar mistake, with equally devastating effects, by becoming drawn into a sexual relationship, because of his own oedipal desires, with an abused and damaged object. His attraction to her is oedipal because she is the exclusive possession of the man who has abused her.

Gittes is profoundly shocked to discover that the woman he is protecting (Evelyn Mulray), whom he is in love with and wants to possess, had an incestuous relationship with her father (Noah Cross) during her adolescence, which produced a daughter. This admission is brutally beaten out of her, although there is some evidence that Gittes may, or could, already know this but is unable to face it, preferring to turn a blind eye to this sexual couple of father and daughter. Like Oedipus, he sees and does not see an incestuous crime (Steiner, 1985). He knows that her father (Noah Cross, whose name suggests duplicity, as well as the oedipal crossroads) is a powerful, ruthless and destructive man who believes that he possesses his daughter, and was in fact a business partner of her husband (reinforcing the sense of incestuous ties).

responseSet.title: A History of Child Psychoanalysis: Claudine Geissmann and Pierre Geissmann. London and New York: Routledge. 1998. Pp. 352
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A History of Child Psychoanalysis

Claudine Geissmann and Pierre Geissmann. London and New York: Routledge. 1998. Pp. 352

Renata Gaddini

Renata Gaddini

Via dei Monti Parioli 40,00197 Rome

Renata Gaddini  

Investigating the birth of child psychoanalysis and the nature and identity of child psychoanalysts seems to have been one of the authors’ main motivations for embarking on many years of work involving both the study of documents and direct contacts with analytical groups throughout the world, with a view to reconstructing the collective history and hence also the collective memory of child psychoanalysis.

The introduction quotes Freud's conviction that child psychoanalysis could exist and his statement that it had actually been conducted since the early years of the century:

the … child on whom the experiment was ventured … has since grown into a healthy and capable young man, who has passed through his puberty irreproachably, in spite of some severe psychical traumas … From the point of view of theory, [the] value [of child analyses] is beyond question (1926, pp. 214-5).

Vienna, the city that received Freud as a child and was the cradle of psychoanalysis, has been a breeding ground for geniuses of all kinds, especially in the arts, but, as the authors point out (p. 3), the unconscious is not on that account Viennese, even in children. Born in Vienna, however, psychoanalysis grew up in the United Kingdom and spread and propagated in other countries, especially those of Latin America, starting with Argentina. Bion (1961) adduces the myth of the Tower of Babel as the origin of the development of language and hence of consciousness (K), which, according to the authors, is somehow punished because of its status as an instinctual drive.

The volume begins with a preface by Anne-Marie Sandler and Hanna Segal and a foreword by Serge Lebovici, who reviews the beginnings of child psychoanalysis in the Wednesday meetings in Freud's apartment, at which, among other things, his children's dreams were discussed (perhaps they were also interpreted to the children, as was the practice of Little Hans's father).

The history falls into three parts. The first—‘The day before yesterday: beginnings in Vienna (1905–20)’—outlines the origins of child psychoanalysis in Vienna and introduces the thought of Freud and Jung, including their divergent views, as well as the figure of Hermine Hug-Hellmuth, a devoted and obstinate disciple of Freud and pioneer of the clinical application of psychoanalysis to children, whose tragic end certainly did not facilitate the acceptance of her innovative ideas.

responseSet.title: ‘“Dreams that Turn Over a Page”: Integration Dreams with Paradoxical Regressive Content’ by Jean-Michel Quinodoz
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘“Dreams that Turn Over a Page”

Integration Dreams with Paradoxical Regressive Content’ by Jean-Michel Quinodoz

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

15 Arden Road, London N3 3AB
williams@psyc.demon.co.uk

Paul Williams  

Jean-Michel Quinodoz's paper was published in Part 2 (Int. J. Psychoanal., 1999, 80: 225-238), and was placed on the IJP web-site (http:/www.ijpa.org) several weeks earlier for discussion. The subject of his paper is what he terms ‘dreams that turn over a page’, the primitive content of which frightens the dreamer, although the analyst may see them as a sign of progress in psychic integration. Quinodoz's thesis is illustrated by clinical examples. Such seemingly regressive dreams represent progress because they occur at points in the transference when projective identification gives way to introjection. The reintegration of previously expelled fragments causes anxiety but also gives the dreamer a sense of inner cohesion.

One of Quinodoz's patients consistently arrived late and never closed the waiting room door. Attempts to interpret the unconscious transference reasons for her claustrophobic symptom were in vain. One day the patient began to come on time and to close the door. She subsequently brought a dream, the manifest content of which had made her think that she would stay mad for the rest of her life: she had dreamed that she was being locked up in a prison from which she would never again emerge. After reaching the stage of overcoming her claustrophobic symptom in the transference relationship she paradoxically produced a dream of claustrophobic anxiety. Another analysand, in the process of resolving difficulties in his emotional and sexual life, had a dream that made him despair.

He dreamed with intense anxiety that he had to catch a train but could not manage it, and had to go in a lift, but was unable to do this either. ‘I miss everything! I shall never get anywhere! I am not making any progress!’ he told Quinodoz, adding that he had tidied up his house and written letters he had been putting off for a long time. Just as things were falling into place in his mind, he had become able to see how intensely he believed that he would never succeed in reaching such a point. A further patient was so fused with Quinodoz in fantasy that there was nothing inside or outside herself but vague, blurred perceptions. When she eventually emerged from this state she had an anxiety dream in which she was wearing spectacles that disturbed her vision: ‘I seemed to be squashing my nose up against you so that I could not see you’, she said. The patient used an anxiety dream to represent to herself the disturbance of relational perception that she was in the process of overcoming.

responseSet.title: Absolute Zero: A Man Who Doubts His Own Love …
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Absolute Zero

A Man Who Doubts His Own Love …

Priscilla Roth

Priscilla Roth

12 Parkhill Road
London NW3 2YN

Priscilla Roth  

In this paper the author explores the relationship, first formulated by Freud, between depression and the experience of losing one's feeling of being able to love. She emphasises the narcissistic organisation underlying depression and depressive anxieties, and argues that the defences used to protect against such anxieties frequently produce the very states of mind they are supposed to be defending against. Using clinical material from a patient in analysis, she describes the way in which a combination of idealisation and a subtle, pervasive contempt for objects creates a brittle, sham relationship with the analyst, and that when this sham relationship is threatened, the patient can feel a catastrophic sense of desolation. Such events threaten the analysis, and often create correspondingly great anxiety in the analyst. At such times the analyst may be tempted to return the analytic relationship to the earlier, seemingly less disturbing state. The author argues that this would be a mistake, because it perpetuates the patient's despair about being able to engage only in fraudulent, ultimately empty relationships, and his fear that the analyst is unable to recognise this despair and to help him with it.

[[RunningHead]]

Siblings in the Unconscious and Psychopathology

Vamik D. Volkan and Gabrielle Ast. Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press. 1997. Pp. 184 + xiv

Peter Neubauer

Peter Neubauer

33 East 70 Street, New York, NY 10021

Peter Neubauer  

The reader can rest assured that the title of this book does not refer to an encyclopaedic exploration of the sibling experience and its multiple influence on psychopathology. In the introduction the authors make it clear that they address the ‘mental representation of childhood siblings … in adult patients’ internal worlds, and the role they play in psychopathology' (p. xi, my italics). They are not interested in studying normal or transient conflicts about rivalry, envy and jealousy among siblings. They focus on the role of the unconscious fantasies in adult life as the necessary entrance into the inner mental life as being the pivotal mode of the analytic intervention. They never fail to assert that they are examining sibling ‘representations’. They do not dwell on conscious fantasies, day-dreams or overt behaviour, but study the repressed conflicts.

It is this traditional psychoanalytic position that they never abandon. It leads them directly to the significance of unconscious fantasies, to which they assign an early chapter. Here, too, they restrict the field of exploration, defining unconscious fantasies as being repressed conscious fantasies or day-dreams. They do not include Freud's assumption of primary unconscious fantasies that have been unconscious all along, as an expression of the drives. The authors then state that the repressed day-dreams have to follow primary process thinking, but, and there is the interesting characteristic of unconscious fantasies, they still follow their organised story-like quality. The authors do not pursue here the notion of whether there is an organised component of the unconscious or whether some aspects of the conscious day-dreams are repressed in such a way as to retain components of the secondary process. In recognition of these and other factors, the authors recognise that the metapsychological formulation about unconscious fantasies have been rather difficult.

Since their work examines the consequence of unconscious fantasies about siblings in adult life, these fantasies have to be enduring, that is, they do not yield to developmental trransformations. They have become independent of the earliest constellations, which position the sibling experience within the context of the parental primary relationship. After this the authors describe how the importance of the sibling experience in the earlier pre-oedipal, oedipal and later developmental stages, are ‘contaminated’ by the dominant role played by the parents' representation. They argue that sibling experiences, in their own right, become key elements in an adult's psychic life when they are included in specific unconscious fantasies, since they, like black holes in space, absorb the surrounding developmental issues.

responseSet.title: W. R. Bion. War Memoirs 1917–19: Francesca Bion. London: Karnac Books. 1997. Pp. 312
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

W. R. Bion. War Memoirs 1917–19

Francesca Bion. London: Karnac Books. 1997. Pp. 312

Rosalind Leigh

Rosalind Leigh

Psychotherapy Services, A14 Archway Wing
Whittington Hospital, London N19 5NF

Rosalind Leigh  

No one who has not been through the chaos and carnage of the First World War on the Western Front in France and Belgium can really understand what it must have been like. This book is an addition to the already quite substantial collection of Bion's autobiographical writings and

410

deals specifically with this period of his life. Edited by his widow, Francesca Bion, it consists of three sections.

The major portion of the book, to me the most interesting, is a Diary. This was written not during the war itself but immediately afterwards in 1919, after Bion went up to Queen's College, Oxford. This Diary, a chronological account of his service in France between June 1917 and January 1919, is written for his mother. During the war itself Bion found himself unable to write to his parents at all, although at the time not quite knowing why. One assumes that his experiences were too indigestible, too incommunicable, to be put into words for his family to read. The Diary is an attempt to fill in these gaps, belatedly to provide for his mother some kind of account of what he had been doing and what had been happening to him.

The second section of the book is a short ‘Commentary’ written in 1972 after Bion read the Diary again, as he says, for the first time since it had been originally written. The Commentary takes the form of a conversation about the contents of the Diary between a person he calls ‘Myself’, the Bion of the then present day aged 75, and a character called ‘Bion’ who is the 21-year-old of 1919. The third section of the book was written in 1958 after his memory was jogged by a train journey with Francesca through Northern France. It is an account of the Battle of Amiens and is written in the third person about the experiences of a ‘Captain Bion DSO’. It is a much more literary narrative, almost reading as a short story. It breaks off in the middle of a sentence. As Francesca reminds us, Bion was at the time also engaged in the writing of Learning from Experience so that other interests were obviously more at the forefront of his mind. Occupied with this and his subsequent psychoanalytic writings, Bion never returned to complete the manuscript. The Battle of Amiens of course takes up quite a lot of the original Diary, unread by Bion at this point in time, and it is fascinating to compare these two versions. A final short section ‘Aftermath’ is written by Bion's daughter, the late Parthenope Bion.

responseSet.title: Psychopathologie de l'anorexie mentale [The psychopathology of anorexia nervosa]: Bernard Brusset. Paris: Dunod. 1998. Pp. 229
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychopathologie de l'anorexie mentale [The psychopathology of anorexia nervosa]

Bernard Brusset. Paris: Dunod. 1998. Pp. 229

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence

61 Godolphin Road, London W12 8JN

Marilyn Lawrence  

This book is one of the latest in the Psychismes series, edited by Didier Anzieu. Its author, Professor Bernard Brusset, is a psychiatrist and psychoanalyst with many years' experience of treating and writing about patients with eating disorders. It is a sad fact, related I think to the curious divide

604

between French and British psychoanalysis, that I had never come across Professor Brusset's work before being invited to review this important book.

Comprehensive psychoanalytic accounts of anorexia are rare. Rarer still are accounts that allow themselves to be informed by and to integrate medical and non-psychoanalytic knowledge and research on these puzzling conditions that lie on the boundary between psyche and soma. In his foreword, the author refers to the institutional separation between neurology and psychiatry that took place in France in 1969 and the struggle that ensued between competing explanations for mental illness. The neuro-endocrine dysfunction present in anorexia led to a serious confusion within psychiatry as to its origins and in Brusset's assessment, to treatment that was at best paternalistic and at worst sadistic. He therefore puts himself firmly on the side of attempting to understand anorexia psychoanalytically. However, he acknowledges the huge contribution made to this field of enquiry by non-analytic authors such as the pioneering American psychiatrist, Hilde Bruch, and his own researches finally bring him full circle to considerations of the role of starvation in the production of endorphins.

The book is in three parts; the first is a clinical description of anorexia nervosa and a theoretical account of its origins and development. This latter includes a most interesting account of cultural factors and in particular the significance of the fast. The second part presents his psychoanalytic understanding of anorexia, with the use of rich clinical material, largely derived from two detailed cases. The third part covers the addictive dimension of anorexia, in which the author argues that anorexia nervosa in girls has much in common with drug addiction in boys and is in itself a kind of endogenous drug addiction.

responseSet.title: Response
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Mervin Glasser

Mervin Glasser

10 Nutley Terrace
London NW3 5SB

Mervin Glasser  

Dear Sir,

I see no cause for dispute in two authors having different approaches to the same type of behaviour, particularly if they come at it from different angles, and it is not uncommon for their respective views to overlap. For example, Dr Meloy's category of ‘Affective’ violence is very similar to my category of ‘Self-preservative violence’. We differ substantially on the other category of violence which we identify, namely, his ‘predatory’ and my ‘sadomasochistic’ violence.

Needless to say, Dr Meloy or any other author, is entitled to favour his approach and Dr Meloy makes a substantial contribution to the field in his book (1988). But I do not think it relevant here to embark on comparing the respective values of our differing approaches. My concern then is to limit myself to making corrections to misunderstandings in Dr Meloy's comments on my paper.

My impression is that Dr Meloy has read my paper rather hastily and therefore missed points I make. For example, as regards my ‘unwillingness to … consider empirical research’, Dr Meloy on re-reading my paper will note that I refer in my first paragraph to a study at the Portman Clinic of acts of violence (p. 887). Since I was not considering this study as such, I regarded it as inappropriate to elaborate that the study restricted itself to empirical data that was obtained from clinical material concerned with exploring in depth the psychodynamic nature of violence; and it did not interest itself with psychobiological issues except to characterize the fundamental nature of Self-preservative violence. I have elaborated the methodology of this research elsewhere (Glasser, 1994). Schachter & Luborsky in their thoughtful paper conclude that they ‘believe that the clinical and the research approach each have their values and that each has contributed to the development of psychoanalysis’ (1998, p. 967). Our study has in fact sought to combine both.

Dr Meloy criticizes me for stating that ‘predatory violence’ is not violence (p. 890). If he were to read my paper more carefully he would see on what grounds and in what context I would consider predatory behaviour as being complex and that violence is only one aspect of it, the nature of which I would categorize as Self-preservative. This clarification depends crucially, in my opinion, on the differentiation—which I make explicitly in the same paragraph—between activity and violence. Dr Meloy would see then that I do not leave it to an ‘exclamation point’ to convince the reader.

responseSet.title: Cassandra's Daughter: A History of Psychoanalysis in Europe and America: Joseph Schwartz. London: Allen Lane, Penguin Press. 1999. Pp. 339
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Cassandra's Daughter: A History of Psychoanalysis in Europe and America

Joseph Schwartz. London: Allen Lane, Penguin Press. 1999. Pp. 339

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

37, Woodsome Road, London NW5 1SA

David Tuckett  

Joseph Schwartz's short book is enjoyable and daring: in scope and in subject matter. An American who initially trained as a physicist, Schwartz worked for fifteen years in mental health research before becoming a psychotherapist and a professional writer in England. He writes well and his book is easy to read and to study. He is writing for a general audience beyond the psychoanalytic enclave.

Schwartz believes that psychoanalysis (for him the attempt over the past hundred years to understand the structure and dynamics of the inner world of the experiencing human being) is arguably the most important intellectual development of the twentieth century. He is aware that this belief is controversial (particularly in medical, psychology and establishment circles) to the extent that the Freudian project has repeatedly been attacked as a swindle. He is interested in the prejudices he considers to make it so: broadly, these are naïve and inaccurate ideas as to what constitutes a valid claim to knowledge in the study of human subjective experience as well as a deep and entrenched terror of all human subjectivity and emotion. Intriguingly he argues how the latter is evident in the right-wing political attacks on psychoanalysis, whether in 1920s Hungary, 1930s Germany or 1970s Argentina.

For Schwartz ‘science’ is the effort to build a body of secure understanding based on knowledge rather than belief (see also Britton, 1995). His argument to show that psychoanalysis is a scientific project includes a novel strand of argument about what has often been called the ‘ecological fallacy”—the attempt to analyse phenomena at the wrong level.

He suggests psychoanalysis is and must be a science in the sense that it shares with other disciplines the aspiration to understand human subjectivity in material terms, namely in terms of lived experience. He considers that the past successes of physics and the beauty and simplicity of its explanations have dwarfed the way in which we think about other sciences. Thus, only fifty years ago molecular biology was belittled for its lack of precision, its ‘stamp-collecting’ approach, its lack of generality and its apparent lack of (mathematical) rigour. However, the spectacular results of molecular biology since these charges were made have demonstrated a different model for scientific success from that of physics. The successes of molecular biology, argues Schwartz, have been descriptive. ‘Understanding has been advanced, not by the statement of precise quantitative laws of motion, but by describing in detail the specific structures and sequences of events occurring in the biological processes of interest”. The reason for this is simple: ‘The phenomena of biology are historical phenomena’ (p. 6). The genetic material of living organisms is the complex result of years of accumulated evolutionary changes. ‘Such complexity cannot be understood in terms of frameworks erected for simpler forms of matter … Complexity generates new phenomena that need to be understood in their own terms’ (pp. 6-7).

responseSet.title: Adolescent Breakdown and Beyond: Moses Laufer. London: Karnac. 1997. Pp 156
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Adolescent Breakdown and Beyond

Moses Laufer. London: Karnac. 1997. Pp 156

Ann Lévy

Ann Lévy

4 Cours des Longs Prés, 92100 Boulogne

Ann Lévy  

Adolescent Breakdown and Beyond is a very readable, concise book, containing many clinical examples, which make it easily accessible not only to psychoanalysts involved or interested in the treatment of adolescents, but to the allied caring professions, as well as to parents, and even adolescents themselves. This is, in itself, a praiseworthy achievement.

The book is divided into two sections. The first of these is composed of five introductory chapters, homogeneous in style, and complementary in content, written by staff or former staff members of the Brent Adolescent Centre, who define and deal with ‘The Adolescent and Developmental Breakdown’, its assessment, treatment and limitations. The second section contains four presentations that were discussed by various professionals from different centres and constitute the proceedings of the ‘Conference on Adolescent Breakdown and Beyond’, which was held in October 1995, for the Brent Adolescent Centre is also a Centre for Research into Adolescent Breakdown whose aims include: the prevention of breakdown, an ongoing investigation into the field, and the possibility for exchange with other colleagues with different points of view, based on different experiences.

In the opening chapter, ‘Developmental breakdown in adolescence: problems of understanding and helping’ (and in Chapter 6, pp. 75-87), Moses Laufer responds to the task of differentiating ‘between normal stress and signs of developmental breakdown’ (p. 4) that lead to an increase in attempted suicide, ‘sudden signs of mental illness’ such as schizophrenia, anorexia, drug addiction, severe depression, sexual abnormality and violence in the adolescent. He feels that ‘there are not only new stresses following physical sexual maturity but that … adolescence is a time of special vulnerability to breakdown’ (p. 4).

responseSet.title: Les raisons de l'irrationnel [The reasons of the irrational]: Michel Neyraut. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France, Collection Le fil rouge. 1997. Pp. 294
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Les raisons de l'irrationnel [The reasons of the irrational]

Michel Neyraut. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France, Collection Le fil rouge. 1997. Pp. 294

Bernard Lemaigre

Bernard Lemaigre

23, rue des Martyrs, 75009 Paris

Bernard Lemaigre  

By virtue of the originality of the views expressed and the vigour of the author's thought, Les raisons de l'irrationnel is an important book. In his survey of the field of psychoanalysis, Michel Neyraut opts for a consideration of thought and reason in their relations with the irrational; this is an unusual approach even for those interested in the functioning of the psychic apparatus, although Bion and Lacan also followed it. In an earlier book, on the ‘logics of the unconscious’ (Neyraut, 1978), the author took advantage of the fact that ‘our age invites us to the banquet of logics’ (p. 11) to attack the ‘stubborn dogma of a single reason, a universal logic and an unalterable consensus’ (p. 11f.). Whether the form of this single reason is that of statistics or of divine providence, this dogma in his view leads to the elimination of the ‘unconscious’. Neyraut recently expanded his reflections on reason, memory and their links with unconscious processes in an interdisciplinary debate between biology and psychoanalysis based on a recent book by Edelman (1992). Here he expresses the same conviction, which concerns the need to resist any inclination to ‘confuse human memory with the syntactic and coded models that are only too bound up with a single reason’ (Neyraut, 1996, p. 50). In case the reader is surprised by the theoretical character of this book, it should be added that it in fact constitutes a solidly based summing up, by way of a metapsychology of thought, of the reflections of a French psychoanalyst on the whole of his psychoanalytic practice, which has extended over many years and covers a wide clinical field. However, let us return to the issue of reason.

Even if psychoanalytic thought possesses no particular instruments for criticising the foundations of reason, whose existence it accepts, it does, in Neyraut's view, broaden the working field of this reason and highlights the diversity of ‘reasons’. Appealing to ‘psychic causality’, psychoanalytic thought introduces a range of absolutely heterogeneous entities as ‘reasons’ or ‘causes’ of the irrational that it encounters; they are heterogeneous because borrowed from such widely differing fields as biology, linguistics, the phylogenetic notion of innate qualities, and culture and civilisation (p. 1f.). Although heterogeneous, they are also overdetermining, because the compromise underlying the formations of the unconscious results from multiple, conflictual, contradictory causes from different ages. This may even result in a reversal of perspective: ‘Any would-be scientific approach that allows for neither the form nor the content of the unconscious must be deemed irrational’ (p. 40). In addition, Neyraut emphasises the novelty and scandalous specificity of psychoanalysis, ‘which is like nothing else that may already be known and constitutes a break with all established forms of knowledge’ (p. 49)

responseSet.title: Intimate Attachments: Toward a New Self Psychology: Morton Shane, Estelle Shane and Mary Gales. New York: Guilford Press. 1997. Pp. 242
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intimate Attachments: Toward a New Self Psychology

Morton Shane, Estelle Shane and Mary Gales. New York: Guilford Press. 1997. Pp. 242

Allen Siegel

Allen Siegel

111 North Wabash Avenue, Chicago, IL 60602

Allen Siegel  

Ensconced behind the barricade of Freud's canon, psychoanalysis has been slow to embrace challenges from within and without. The internal challenges have come in the form of attempts either to question or extend Freud's ideas. Despite the fact that analysts whose contributions fell outside the canon were treated as heretics rather than as participants in a scientific discourse, brave analysts have occasionally emerged to challenge our diffident field with new ideas. Morton Shane, Estelle Shane and Mary Gales are three such courageous analysts and their book, Intimate Attachments: Toward a New Self Psychology, clearly and directly challenges basic psychoanalytic assumptions.

The book opens with the analysis of a traumatised woman for whom no sign of transference, which they define as the re-enactment with the analyst of old conflicted and traumatic experiences, emerges. Instead, the authors assert that the woman's analytic experience is marked by a never before known feeling of safety. They challenge the reader with the nagging question of whether this really can be an analysis when so little transference is evident and so much that is new and positive is experienced. Gales and the Shanes answer this provocative question in the first chapter, which then serves as a guide to the new self-psychology they elaborate in the first half of their book; abundant clinical material spanning the life cycle fills the second half.

Intimate Attachments is an iconoclastic work. With origins in Bowlby's attachment theory and in some aspects of Kohut's work, the authors' new theory challenges classical psychoanalytic ideas and Kohut's original psychology of the self as well by de-emphasising the notion of psychoanalytic cure effected by an interpretive process. Instead they advance the concept of analytic cure effected by the provision of a missing essential relationship, a subject of current debate within the field of self-psychology (Siegel, 1999).

In this review, I summarise the major tenets of the authors' new theory and critique their views about several basic psychoanalytic assumptions. Among these assumptions are: the authors' new definition of emotional health with its related question of, ‘What are the proper goals of an analysis?’; their new definition of transference and its related question, ‘What are the contents of the unconscious and what is their role in an analysis?’; and ‘What is the nature of psychoanalytic treatment itself?

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Of A Mentally Retarded Man
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Of A Mentally Retarded Man

Michael Kilchenstein MD

Michael Kilchenstein

1013 Boyce Avenue
Ruxton
MD 21204

Michael Kilchenstein  

In this paper, the author discusses the psychoanalytically informed treatment of a 25-year-old patient, who at the beginning of treatment was severely developmentally retarded. The patient showed repetitive behaviours (tapping his fingers, tying and retying his shoelaces); visual and auditory hallucinations, delusions, vague to absent ego boundaries; inability to do simple maths, tell the time, read or write; and angry vulgar outbursts accompanied by occasional throwing of objects. He was bewildering and unresponsive to various psychological and physical treatments that had been performed since his infancy. The patient had been examined by a variety of medical and psychological specialists, but he seemed to belong in no particular place in the system of health-care providers. All courses of drugs that were tried proved ineffective. This author presents clinical material from a psychoanalytic psychotherapy that resulted in remarkable improvement in the patient's capacity for self-reflective thought as well as in his capacities for more mature object relationships and work. More specifically, the author discusses aspects of the treatment he thinks were most pivotal: (1) the interpretation of sensation (autistic) states; (2) the use of countertransference derived understandings of the patient's intra- and intersubjective experiences to guide his interventions; (3) the use of sensation-based communication in a verbal form of play.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Putting into Words, Putting to Rest and Putting Aside the Ancestors

How an Analysand who was heir to the Armenian Genocide of 1915 Worked Through Mourning

Janine Altounian

Janine Altounian

18, avenue Général Leclerc
75014 Paris

Janine Altounian  

The author discusses the intergenerational psychic transmission of collective trauma on the basis of her personal experience as a descendant of victims of the Armenian genocide of 1915. She shows how the processes of transmission are encumbered within a diaspora community such as hers by the incorporation of objects in the throes of mourning, the invalidation of prohibitions by murder-become-law, and lack of differentiation between the sexes. A parallel is drawn between the characteristic secrecy of the genocidal project on the part of the perpetrators and the sense of illegitimacy of the victims' descendants, exacerbated in the case of the Armenian catastrophe by the refusal of the state that inherited the genocide to confess to it and consequently its erasure from Western consciousness. The author describes how she was enabled to emerge from confinement in the trauma by her French schooling and her analysis, and subsequently became able to work through mourning and writing, by divulging the secret and in particular the publication of her father's deportation diary. The written text is seen as a shroud in which the dead can finally be interred. Presenting an episode from her schooldays, she demonstrates the importance of her immersion in French culture in allowing her to achieve the necessary linguistic and psychic distance from her heritage.

Louise De Urtubey  

Both imagination and belief have been the subject of a number of philosophical studies (Hobbes, Bergson, Taine for the first, and especially Kant for the second, for example). Although psychoanalytical literature has not disdained them, they have been generally regarded as unrelated to the analytical situation and have been linked, rather, to creativity and art.

responseSet.title: John Frosch (1909-1999)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

John Frosch (1909-1999)

William Frosch

William Frosch

525 East 68th St, Box 171
New York
NY 10021

William Frosch  

John Frosch, the founding Editor of the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, died on 2 February, 1999, six weeks after his eighty-ninth birthday.

Jack, as he was known to all, was born and brought up on Orchard Street in the teeming and turbulent Lower East Side of New York. His parents, along with his older siblings, had left their European shtetl for the promise of the new world: this proved to be a crowded walk-up tenement flat and a pushcart from which his irascible, scholarly talmudist father sold the butter and eggs he stored in the basement. Encouraged and supported by his eldest brother who had already become a physician, Jack went on to City College and to medical school, briefly in Berlin, where he witnessed the early impact of Hitler, and then moving to and graduating from the University of Bern in 1934. His doctoral thesis on ‘The experimental production of dreams in hypnosis’ was the basis of his first paper, which appeared in Nervenarzt the following year, 1935 (9: 170-82).

Jack interned at the Morrisania City Hospital (1934–36) in New York, where his eldest brother (my father) was studying medicine, and spent a year in Neurology at the Montefiore Hospital (1936–37), which then specialised in the care of patients with neurological illnesses. He trained in psychiatry at the New York State Psychiatric Institute (1937–38) and at Bellevue Hospital (1938–40), and in psychoanalysis at the New York Psychoanalytic Institute (graduating in 1944). With the exception of a two-year stint as Professor and Executive Officer of the nascent Department of Psychiatry at the Albert Einstein College of Medicine (1954–55), he remained at Bellevue and the New York University Medical School for more than twenty-five years, where he became Professor and directed the residency training programme in psychiatry. He then moved to the Downstate Medical Center of SUNY (State University of New York) in 1965, and served as Director of the Department of Psychiatry of the Brookdale Hospital Center from 1967 to 1977, becoming Emeritus Director in 1978. Following his ‘retirement’ he continued to teach at NYU-Bellevue, and at both the New York and Downstate (now New York University) Psychoanalytic Institutes.

responseSet.title: Internet Discussion Review ‘Problems Of Female Sexuality: The Defensive Function Of Certain Phantasies About The Body’ By Loredana Micati Squitieri
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Internet Discussion Review ‘Problems Of Female Sexuality

The Defensive Function Of Certain Phantasies About The Body’ By Loredana Micati Squitieri

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

15 Arden Road, London N3 3AB
williams@dial.pipex.com

Paul Williams  

Micati Squitieri's paper appeared in Part 4, Volume 80, and was placed on the website (http://www.ijpa.org) several weeks earlier for discussion. In brief, the author discusses early mental representations of the body that may be chosen by a female child as a defence against developmental difficulties, particularly ‘primitive agonies’. In cases of women in whom representations of a fragile self predominate, the body is not only felt as being a mysterious, receptive cavity, but also as an unreliable organism. Where the self is perceived as fragile, the body also tends to be thought of as being so fragile as to increase the self's fragility. Following this hypothesis, the inner representation of the precariousness of the self tends to take the form of an unreliable anatomy and, in turn, the perception of a delicate, mysterious anatomy sharpens the feeling of the fragility of the self. The female body may be experienced as a wounded body (Argentieri, 1982, 1985), which, through an opening over which there is no control (Bernstein, 1990), may lose its inner contents and let in dangerous aggressors. The vaginal opening may also be seen in a way that triggers persecutory anxieties. A defensive phantasy (Micati, 1992) created as protection against these painful perceptions may be that

of, secretly and guiltily, having some qualities of the male body (robustness, strength, solidity), represented by reassuring male sexual organs.

Micati Squitieri summarises how Freud (1925) links female psychic development to the meaning that the female child is able to give to the discovery of her anatomy. This meaning is one of a lack. When the little girl realises her lack of a penis she replaces the penis wish with the wish to have a child by the father. When she enters the phallic phase the little girl is a ‘little man’, the vagina being ignored by both sexes. Horney by contrast (1926) emphasises the positive aspects of female sexuality and wonders if male envy of pregnancy could underlie male depreciation of women. Klein (1932) thinks that little girls have an early knowledge of the existence of an opening in their genitals and that because of the anxiety connected with the interior of their body, this knowledge undergoes repression. Later (1945), Klein reaffirms the existence of the representation of the vagina in the infantile unconscious. Even according to Jones, the obscurity of the vagina in childhood is partially related to the depth of early female anxieties connected with this organ. In Jones's view ‘[the girl] is concerned more with the inside of her body than with the outside’ (1935, p. 265)

responseSet.title: Shame in Context: Susan B. Miller. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1996. Pp. 241
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Shame in Context

Susan B. Miller. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1996. Pp. 241

Andrew Morrison

Andrew Morrison

32 Hawthorn Street, Cambridge, MA 02138

Andrew Morrison  

In her fine exploration and examination of current thinking about shame, Susan Miller has attempted to present major themes and contributions from the burgeoning shame literature, comparing differing perspectives and evaluating salient issues regarding this seminal affect. Miller writes clearly and vividly, with felicitous phrasing that frequently captures the particular flavour of a given construct or clinical moment. She is generous in her acknowledgement of, and respect for, the contributions of contemporary writers on shame, and is indefatigable in her attempts to sort out differences, implications and problems with specific theoretical positions.

In addition to several published articles on shame, Miller has contributed a previous book on the subject (1985). The current work reflects her continuing struggle to put shame into an appropriate perspective. She expresses concern that the plethora of writings on shame tends to extend it too much prominence in the pantheon of human experience, and this book seems as much an effort to ‘cut shame down to size’ as it is to promulgate any particular theory.

Miller approaches her task in orderly fashion. After first setting forth some major issues regarding shame, she offers three clinical contexts from which to study the subject, with a separate chapter following each one providing detailed clinical examples. First, she examines shame's role in obsessive-compulsive conditions followed by studies of shame ‘in the background’ (her conceptualisation of narcissism and narcissistic disturbances), and then shame ‘in the foreground’ (represented by masochism—moral and otherwise). While acknowledging other clinical conditions from which to consider shame, she utilises these categories well to explore the themes that are to her of most pressing concern.

In her first chapter, Miller sets out some basic shame issues, including the sense, running throughout the book, that shame has been put forth too globally as a fundamental source of human distress.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychanalyse des comportements sexuels violents [Psychoanalysis of violent sexual behaviour]

Claude Balier. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France, Collection Le fil rouge. 1996. Pp. 252

Sidney Cohen

Sidney Cohen

3, rue de Lionne, 38000 Grenoble

Sidney Cohen  

Sexual violence is a highly topical subject of concern to both the medico-psychological and the legal professions. The main issues are the responsibility of the perpetrators of sexual assaults and the possibility of therapy for them. Our approach to this problem has changed greatly in the last century, these individuals now being seen no longer as monsters but as complex psychopathological personalities. Psychoanalysis has made a substantial contribution to this development, by regarding perversion as a universal model and no longer solely as a pathology and by placing it at the root of unconscious functioning, sexual life and psychic life in general.

Psychoanalysis therefore has a great deal to say in the present debate and, in order for its voice to be heard, needs a protagonist sufficiently involved in work with those who commit violent sex crimes. Claude Balier's qualifications are exemplary in this respect: as a psychoanalyst and physician in charge of a prison psychiatric unit that he himself created and organised, he concentrated initially on the problem of violent behaviour in general, to which he devoted an important earlier book (Balier, 1988, Psychanalyse des comportements violents, Paris: Presses Univ. France), turning subsequently to the specific topic of sexual violence, which he now deals with at length.

The two facets of his work throw right on the overall problem of violent acts and at the same time provide valuable information on their sexual form. As the titles of his two books show, behaviour is the principal form of manifestation of these pathologies, in which the act supplants a psychic life ‘that is obliterated’ (p. 21). The author thus distinguishes the classical concept of enactment, which results from psychic conflict and is therefore meaningful, from ‘behavioural acting out’, which is a much more invasive and systematised mode of functioning.

Balier goes on to describe the expression of a ‘madness’ or ‘disproportion’ in these subjects, involving a major narcissistic or identity disorder.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Non-interpretive mechanisms in psychoanalytic therapy

Larry Hirschhorn

Larry Hirschhorn

Philadelphia
PA 19104
Lhirschhorn@mail.cfar.com

Larry Hirschhorn  

Dear Sir,

I want to draw attention to another framework that may help clarify what Stern et al. call ‘moments of meaning’. These moments can be conceptualized as double binds. The double bind, as Gregory Bateson defined it, is the result of two conditions: 1) the context for communication contradicts its content: and 2) the recipient of the communication cannot comment on this contradiction. The familiar example is the mother who tells the child, ‘I love you’, with glaring eyes, or the parent who with a faint sneer offers to help a child with her homework. The glare, the sneer, are the cues that stimulate what Stern et al. call ‘relational knowing’. These cues, which establish the context for communication, contradict the content of the communication; in these examples ‘I love you’, ‘I can help you’. The child cannot comment because she fears the parent. The resulting unremarked contradiction stimulates strong feelings.

Bateson derived his conception from Bertrand Russell's theory of logical types in which one statement is a category marker for a whole set of statements. Thus the statement ‘All the barbers in France’ is a category marker for ‘Jean’, ‘Henry’, ‘Luc’, etc. ‘All the barbers in France’, is of a higher logical type than ‘Jean’, or ‘Henry’. Russell argued that certain familiar paradoxes are resolved if we do not mix statements of different logical types in a single proposition. (‘If the barber shaves everyone who does not shave himself, who shaves the barber?’)

Consider the vignette that Stern et al. present. Molly, the patient, says to the analyst, ‘I have the image of you sitting back and watching me from some superior position’. She then goes on to wonder if, when she was a child, her parents derived sexual pleasure from watching her dance. She concludes with, ‘I wonder if you're looking at me’. Stern et al. note that as the analyst works through her feelings she identifies ‘the related issues of dominance and realized that she felt as if she were being invited either to the superior position or to submit to Molly’.

responseSet.title: The Freudian Calling: Early Viennese Psychoanalysis and the Pursuit of Cultural Science: Louis Rose. Detroit, MI: Wayne State Univ. Press. 1998. Pp. 232
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Freudian Calling: Early Viennese Psychoanalysis and the Pursuit of Cultural Science

Louis Rose. Detroit, MI: Wayne State Univ. Press. 1998. Pp. 232

Maria Bergmann

Maria Bergmann

1136 Fifth Avenue, #14A
New York, NY 10128

Maria Bergmann  

This book, written on a Fulbright fellowship, takes the point of view of a cultural historian observer and describes Freud's first discoveries around the turn of the century and his relationship with his first disciples. Cultural history is traced from the first Wednesday-evening circle in Freud's house in 1902 through the founding of the first Psychoanalytic Society and finally to the Anschluss in 1938. Can a cultural historian going over material already familiar to most psychoanalysts come up with some new and unexpected findings that could make the reading of this book worthwhile? For psychoanalysts already familiar with Jones's and Gay's biographies, and with the Minutes of the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society, edited by Nunberg and Federn, I believe that the answer is nevertheless a qualified yes, if we consider that Freud was unable to see himself and his early disciples within the sociological and cultural perspective that enmeshed them.

The Austrian Constitution of 1867 had raised the hopes of the Jews for social and civic equality, but the Hapsburg monarchy, supported by an autocratic society, allied with the Catholic Church, successfully resisted social change. In studying the history of the parents of Freud's

original circle, most of whom were Jews, the author found that they had already thrown off the shackles of Jewish orthodoxy and begun to participate in a process that led to Jewish emancipation and assimilation. (A very detailed study of the parental background of Freud's early circle can be found in the author's 1992 article in the Psychoanalytic Quarterly.) As is well known, Freud himself had suffered from anti-Semitic discrimination in his effort to be nominated for a professorship, but his indignity was transformed into self-analysis, the spirit of which is described in Schorske's famous 1961 essay, ‘Politics and patricide in Freud's Interpretation of Dreams”. Many of the author's approaches to this period can be viewed as following the pathway of Schorske, to whom his work is dedicated.

[[RunningHead]]

Emprise et satisfaction. Les deux formants de la pulsion [Mastery and satisfaction, the two formants of the drive]

Paul Denis. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. 1997. Pp. 262

Roger Perron

Roger Perron

6 rue Damesme, 75013 Paris

Roger Perron  

The author of this volume finds it surprising that Freud made only occasional reference to the concept of mastery following the interest he took in his early writings. There are various possible explanations, including the wish not to appear to be lending support to Adler or Jung in their desexualising theoretical divergences. This has resulted in a tendency since Freud to disregard the concept. Yet, even if the term itself is quite uncommon in Freud's writings, Denis demonstrates quite brilliantly how it underlies the whole of metapsychology.

This is an ambitious project that undertakes a fundamental reformulation of drive theory and consequent reconsideration of the essentials of metapsychology.

Like many French-speaking analysts, the author contends that the drive is the basis of all psychic functioning. He notes that Freud put forward in succession not two drive theories, as is commonly held, but four: in 1905 he contrasted the sexual drives with the ‘instincts [drives] of mastery’; the antithesis was between the sexual and self-preservative drives in around 1910; then it was sexual drives versus narcissism; and finally, in 1920, there was the dualism of the life and death drives.

1036
