responseSet.title: ‘Listening Psychoanalytically to the Shoah Half a Century on’ by Pérel Wilgowicz
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Listening Psychoanalytically to the Shoah Half a Century on’ by Pérel Wilgowicz

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

15 Arden Road, London N3 3AB
williams@psyc.demon.co.uk

Paul Williams  

Wilgowicz's paper appeared in Part 3, Volume 80, and was placed on the Internet for discussion earlier, on 7 May 1999. The paper aroused strong feelings among discussants as the author addresses a highly sensitive and emotionally charged subject: the repercussions of the Nazi genocide of the Jews on descendants of victims and survivors. There was also debate as to whether her theoretical introduction of a concept of ‘vampiric identification’ was valid or not, and how useful it might be. The personal nature of some of the discussants' responses eventually produced more and longer submissions than has been usual, and regrettably these can only be summarised very briefly here. Anyone interested in this subject is encouraged to download the full discussion from the IJP website archive (http://www.ijpa.org) as it contains a good deal of rich detail not reproduced here.

Wilgowicz discusses in her paper a ‘vampiric’ form of identification in which the subject is stated to be neither alive nor dead, unborn, in an imageless, timeless and spaceless condition, and imprisoned in an earlier generation's traumas. Analysis of such cases must, the author believes, tackle the genocide-induced fantasies of a vampire complex involving infanticide and parricide in addition to the incestuous and parricidal wishes of the Oedipus complex. These ideas are illustrated by a detailed case history of a female patient who carried unrepresentable parental traumas within her to the detriment of her affective and professional life. The patient was gradually released from a vampiric fusion and became able to reappropriate her secondary narcissistic and oedipal identifications, the resulting triangulation opening the way to sublimation and creativity.

Wilgowicz begins the paper by noting how for survivors and descendants of the genocide of the Jews, the Shoah idelibly impressed its stamp on the next generation or generations. In the analysis of such subjects account must be taken of the long-term echoes of the Shoah and blocks in the attainment of subjecthood, particularly of the obstacles to sexuality. The notion of ‘vampirism’ has inspired many works of art and literature. Wilgowicz suggests that a psychic form of vampirism can be present as a low-level undercurrent in psychoanalytic treatments. It is expressed as two beings joined inseparably, unable either to die or to be born. One survives through transfusion, whereas the other is drained of vital essence. A vampire complex based on primitive foetal non-differentiation gives rise to a tension between infanticide and ‘un-birth’, between matricide (or patricide) and ‘un-death’. Vampiric identifications block the structuring of (secondary) narcissism.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Listening Psychoanalytically to the Shoah Half a Century on

Pérel Wilgowicz

Pérel Wilgowicz

7, rue des Blancs-Manteaux
75004 Paris

Pérel Wilgowicz  

The author discusses the repercussions of the Nazi genocide of the Jews on descendants of victims and survivors, with particular reference to identifications. A typical vampiric form of identification is discussed, in which the subject is stated to be neither alive nor dead, unborn, in an imageless, timeless and spaceless condition, and imprisoned in an earlier generation's traumas. Analysis of such cases must, the author shows, tackle the genocide-induced fantasies of a vampire complex involving infanticide and parricide in addition to the incestuous and parricidal wishes of the Oedipus complex. These ideas are illustrated by a case history in which the female patient concerned, although born after the Shoah, carried the unrepresentable parental traumas within her to the detriment of her affective and professional life. The patient was effectively linked with her mother in a deadly circulation through communicating vessels, which was repeated in the transferencel countertransference constellation and resulted in a disavowal of both birth and death, mediated by the genocidal project. The author shows how the analysand was gradually released from this vampiric fusion and became able to reappropriate her secondary narcissistic and oedipal identifications, the resulting triangulation opening the way to sublimation and creativity.

responseSet.title: The Difficult Freedom From a Plan
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Difficult Freedom From a Plan

Tomas Böhm

Tomas Böhm

Norrbackagatan 22
113 41 Stockholm
kaplanbohm@swipnet.se

Tomas Böhm  

The author attempts to describe a tendency among analysts in connection with presenting clinical material to become either too dogmatic or too insecure, either idealising knowledge or uncertainty. Both extremes may also mean idealising intellectualisation. Instead, the author suggests that these tendencies arise as a reaction towards complex psychological strategies from the patient, and that these strategies need to become clarified, instead of the analyst intervening prematurely with the mentioned extreme tendencies. In order to do so, the analyst needs to be free from inner automatic programmes. Two clinical vignettes are described where the analyst's own repression plays a part in his interventions. Freud's technical papers are analysed concerning frame factors and technical recommendations and the author shows how Freud's classical technique is modern in its emphasis on freedom and preliminary truth in interpretations. Freud's papers also seem to agree with Bion's motto ‘without memory or desire’, which the author discusses as the freedom from a programme. He recommends three governing mottoes for the area of interventions, in order not to idealise the image of psychoanalysis and psychoanalysts. He concludes with a third clinical vignette that underlines the connection between classical analysis and modern analysts who emphasise the freedom from idealised programmes within the psychoanalytic frame.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Gender Ideology and Psychological Reality: An Essay on Cultural Reproduction

Melford E. Spiro. New Haven and London: Yale University Press. 1997. Pp. xvii + 220

Robert Paul

Robert Paul

1493 La Vista Road, Atlanta, GA 30324

Robert Paul  

In this book Melford Spiro further advances his lifelong project of clarifying the relationship between cultural, social and psychological domains of human action, addressing two specific questions, a general theoretical one and an empirical, ethnographical one. The theoretical question is that of cultural reproduction: how does it happen that actors in new generations are motivated to put into practice the cultural propositions they have acquired by transmission from their predecessors, with the result that there is a certain degree of stability and continuity of practices in a human social community over time despite the mortality of the people who compose it? To answer this theoretical question, Spiro takes as a test case two ideological propositions about gender held by the men in the village in Burma in which he conducted anthropological fieldwork three decades ago (when Burma, now ‘Myanmar’, was still accessible to outside researchers).

The two ideological propositions Spiro contends are held by Burmese male villagers (he was not in a position to obtain adequate information to generalise about women's beliefs and attitudes on these subjects, although he did collect some data on the subject)

responseSet.title: Donald Levy on ‘Freud among the Philosophers’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Donald Levy on ‘Freud among the Philosophers’

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

1570 Olympus Avenue
Berkeley
CA 94708

Marcia Cavell  

Dear Sir,

I write not so much in defense of Levy's book as in dismay at the tone of Michael Trupp's review of it. Trupp is contemptuous and dismissive, not only of Levy but of philosophy generally. Trupp writes: ‘[such passages] serve …. to justify Freud's well-known derogations of both philosophy and academic psychology as legitimate disciplines for the study of mental life' (p. 618). ‘In this and related discussions Levy repeatedly voices the oddly Procrustean lingo of his discipline’ (p. 618). Trupp speaks of ‘the intellectual cordon sanitaire within whose (conscious) precincts Levy and his philosopher colleagues … most comfortably ply their trade’ (p. 619). It is also simply not the case that Levy, nor even the average philosopher these days, let alone those of us who write about psycho-analysis, identifies the mental with conscious life. Not to know this reveals a hasty reading of Levy's book and unfamiliarity with what is going on in philosophy more generally. As many other psychoanalysts have themselves been saying, psychoanalysis should be reaching out to its neighboring disciplines, not isolating itself from them in the manner of this review.

Sincerly

171

responseSet.title: L'élaboration [Psychic elaboration]: François Duparc. Paris: L'esprit du temps. 1998. Pp. 295. With a foreword by André Green
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

L'élaboration [Psychic elaboration]

François Duparc. Paris: L'esprit du temps. 1998. Pp. 295. With a foreword by André Green

Marie-Lise Roux

Marie-Lise Roux

137 Boulevard St-Michel, 75005 Paris

Marie-Lise Roux  

François Duparc's third book further develops a personal line of thought derived from clinical practice and strictly anchored not only in the work of Freud and his successors but also in the important themes of research on present-day society. The author belongs to the school of French psychoanalytic thought that couples clinical practice with theory, the latter being based on a constant critique of the practice of analytic treatment. The book reflects the issues confronting psychoanalysts today who have to deal with non-neurotic patients—those we describe as ‘difficult cases’—who call into question the validity of a theory that rests on neurotic structures. The problem arises with the various kinds of patients mentioned by Duparc in his extremely lucid survey—namely borderlines, sufferers from psychosomatic conditions, drug addicts, adolescents with deficient psychic structures and manicdepressives. These last inspire the author to some of his most interesting ideas: ‘However brilliant these subjects, whether they are obsessional, schizoid or psychotic, their impaired thought

eventually becomes sterile as a result of autoerotic redundancy, if indeed it does not ultimately plunge into delusion’ (p. 28).

Particular emphasis is placed on the relations between thought and instinctual life, in the context of the continuinng French debate triggered by the positions of Lacan. The author makes no attempt to conceal his debt to the work of André Green, who has written a foreword to this volume and is also the subject of one of Duparc's previous books (Duparc, 1996. André Green. Paris: Presses Univ. France).

The book is divided into three parts. The first comprises a theoretical examination of the practical conclusions of analytic work. This work is then discussed in the second and third parts, on the analytic setting and interpretation respectively.

1) From the introduction on, the author draws attention to the need to take account at all times of a two-fold current in the treatment to support the work of psychic elaboration.

responseSet.title: Affect, Language and Communication: Loose Ends
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Affect, Language and Communication

Loose Ends

Max Hernández

Max Hernández

Paseo de los Virreyes 315-D3
San Isidro
Lima 27

Max Hernández  

The author begins by enquiring whether the three elements of his subject matter are intended to be discussed separately or in terms of the links between them. He goes on to consider the role of language in the development of psychoanalysis from Freud on. Notwithstanding the fruitful mutual influences of linguistics and psychoanalysis, he draws attention to the dangers of a one-sidedly linguistic approach to psychoanalytic theory and clinical practice. Turning to affect, the author reviews Freud's early neurophysiological conceptions and the possible distinctions between affect, emotion and feeling as reflected in the contributions of later workers. The aspects of anxiety, trauma, object relations, transference and infant development are touched upon and quantitative and metapsychological considerations are adduced. The author's starting point for his treatment of communication is Freud's well-known telephone simile. He goes on to discuss subsequent work on mother—baby communication in early childhood, communication as semiology, and communication through acting out and enactment; this section ends with a note on the etymology of the word communication. The author concludes by drawing attention to the importance in analysis of experience, in which the three entities of language, affect and communication are combined. Having failed to answer the question with which he began, he is left with the loose ends of his title.

responseSet.title: Knowledge, Consensus and Uncertainty
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Knowledge, Consensus and Uncertainty

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

1570 Olympus Avenue
Berkeley
CA 94708
cavell@uclink4.berkeley.edu

Marcia Cavell  

Some months ago the editors of this journal asked me if I would undertake a series of short entries of a general sort on philosophical topics germane to current discussions in psychoanalysis. Both authors and topics were left to my discretion. I thought the series was a good idea and gladly agreed to do it. To my surprise and pleasure, all the philosophers I invited accepted. I am only sorry that the series could not be longer as there are other philosophers as well who would have been splendid participants, and other topics I would like to have addressed. The essays that will follow in subsequent issues represent by and large the tradition of analytic philosophy, though this has come in the last few decades to comprise many of the themes we used to associate with the Continental tradition. Future entries, by James Conant, Donald Davison, Pascal Engel, Dagfinn Føllesdal, James Hopkins, Ernest Le Pore, Jeffrey Malpas, Jerome Neu, Brian O'Shaughnessy, Richard Rorty and Richard Wollheim, will address the following topics: intersubjectivity, meaning and language, consciousness and perception, pragmatism, knowledge and belief, norms and nature, metaphor, hermeneutics, truth, self-deception, the emotions. The essay below on knowledge, which will also be the topic of another entry by a different author later on, is the only one in the series that I will write.

responseSet.title: When all is said … A Phenomenological Enquiry into Post-Termination Experience
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

When all is said … A Phenomenological Enquiry into Post-Termination Experience

Paula Conway

Paula Conway

c/o 23 River St.
London EC1R 1XN
dshaw@easynet.co.uk

Paula Conway  

The empirical phenomenological research method (Giorgi, 1985) is used to make a preliminary enquiry into the post-termination experience for a small number of subjects who have completed a psychoanalysis or psychoanalytic psychotherapy. The results are presented and discussed in the context of the existing post-termination literature. While the findings support some current post-termination expectations, such as the capacity for self-analysis and a process of mourning, they potentially refute the idea that these unfold chronologically, with one post-termination stage being contingent upon another. The pre-termination phase emerged as important for these subjects, especially in relation to the termination and post-termination boundaries, which were recalled as being determined largely by the analyst. The findings indicate that the experience of the pre-termination process may have a particular impact on how the analyst is held in mind after the analysis has been terminated.

responseSet.title: ‘Shall we ever know the whole truth about projective identification?’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Shall we ever know the whole truth about projective identification?’

James Grotstein

James Grotstein

522 Dalehurst Avenue
Los Angeles
CA 90024-2516

James Grotstein  

Dear Sir,

I have just read Giovanni Battista Massidda's letter to the Editor, ‘Shall we ever know the whole truth about projective identification?’, in the latest issue of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis (80: 365-7), and I have also read Riccardo Steiner's (80: 367-75) response with great interest. I too should like to contribute what I believe are relevant ideas from that Zeitgeist that may help further to illuminate the mystery of the origins of projective identification.

In an earlier work on the subject I referred to Tausk's description of the origins of the ‘influencing machine’ (Grotstein, 1981). I should like to cite that reference:

The projection of one's body may, then, be traced back to the developmental stage in which one's own body is the goal of the object finding. This must be the time when the infant is discovering his body, part by part, as the outer world, and is still groping for his hands and feet as though they were foreign objects … These disjecta membra are later on pieced together and systematized into a unified whole under the supervision of a psychic unity that receives all sensations of pleasure and pain from these separate parts. This process takes place by means of identification with one's own body … But if the psycho-analytic theories previously employed are correct, this object finding within one's organs, which can be regarding as parts of the outer world only by projection, must be preceded by a stage of identification with a narcissistic libido position, and it is necessary to assume two successive stages of identification and projection … I am, then, assuming the existence of these two successive phases of identification and projection in object finding and object choice within one's own body (1919, pp. 51-2; my italics).

I should now like to cite some of the works of Herman Nunberg, which also seem to be closely related to the concept of projective identification. In ‘On the catatonic attack’ Nunberg states:

The loss of the objects of the external world generates an imperative need to regain them … To this end his own excretions are used as food, which is then subject to the transformation, and again given to the world as excretions. The feces are an object to which it is easiest for the patient to take up the desired libidinal relationship through narcissistic identification. The feces thus become a valuable part of the ego. The partial ego projection which is connected with identification offers the patient a chance to find his way back to the world (Nunberg, 1920, p. 17; my italics).

responseSet.title: ‘The Music of What Happens’ in Poetry and Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘The Music of What Happens’ in Poetry and Psychoanalysis

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

306 Laurel Street
San Francisco
CA 94118

Thomas Ogden  

The author presents a close reading of a Frost poem and a detailed discussion of an analytic session. Using specific examples from the poem and from the analytic session, he then offers some thoughts concerning the relationship between the way he listens to the language of the poem and the way he and his patient speak with and listen to one another. The author illustrates in this reading of the poem and in the way he speaks to his patient that he is no primarily engaged in an effort to unearth what lies ‘behind’ the poem's words and symbols or ‘beneath’ the patient's report of a dream or of a life event. Instead (or perhaps more accurately, in addition), he attempts to listen to the sound and feel of ‘what's going on’, to the ‘music of what happens’. This is achieved to significant degree in the analytic setting by means of the analayst's attending to his own reverie experience.

responseSet.title: ‘Dreams that Turn Over a Page’: Integration Dreams with Paradoxical Regressive Content
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Dreams that Turn Over a Page’

Integration Dreams with Paradoxical Regressive Content

Jean-Michel Quinodoz

Jean-Michel Quinodoz

53 A chemin des Fourches
1223 Cologny (Geneva)

Jean-Michel Quinodoz  

The subject of this paper is ‘dreams that turn over a page’, whose primitive anxiety-inducing content frightens the dreamer, although the psychoanalyst sees them as a sign of progress in psychic integration despite their regressive appearance. This thesis is illustrated by clinical examples. Because such anxiety dreams typically occur at a time of integration, the author considers that the analyst must interpret them as showing that the patient is now able to accept hitherto unrepresentable parts of himself, while the dream content proper should be interpreted at a second stage once the anxiety has subsided. The author postulates that these seemingly regressive dreams are a token of progress because they occur at privileged points in the transference when projective identification is giving way to introjection. The reintegration of previously expelled fragments causes anxiety but also gives the dreamer a sense of inner cohesion, while at the same time accounting for the particular clarity and coherence of these dreams. The author compares his concept of dreams that turn over a page with similar notions in the literature and contends that such dreams retrospectively illuminate changes in the dreamer's intrapsychic conflicts on a more elementary level of unconscious fantasy than the classical approach would suggest.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis, Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy and Supportive Psychotherapy

Contemporary Controversies

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg

21 Bloomingdale Road
White Plains
NY 10605

Otto Kernberg  

The author explores the controversies involving psychoanalytic psychotherapy from conceptual, clinical and educational perspectives. He proposes an integrated concept of psychoanalytic modalities of treatment, and their subdivision into standard psychoanalysis, psychoanalytic psychotherapy and psychoanalytically based supportive psychotherapy. Indications and contra-indications for these therapeutic approaches are outlined in the light of clinical experience and psychoanalytic research on these issues. It is proposed that psychoanalytic institutes teach psychoanalytic psychotherapy to candidates in psychoanalytic training. The author stresses that we now possess a broad spectrum of psychoanalytically based approaches to patients that significantly expand the therapeutic effectiveness of our profession, and thus can strengthen the social impact of psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: The Piggle: Confrontations with Non-Existence in Childhood
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Piggle

Confrontations with Non-Existence in Childhood

Marilyn Charles

Marilyn Charles

325 Wildwood Drive
East Lansing
MI 48823-3154

Marilyn Charles  

The author interweaves pieces from D. W. Winnicott's ‘The Piggle: An Account of the Psychoanalytic Treatment of a Little Girl’ with her own experiences and case material, to explore how confrontations with existencelnon-existence can become terrifying realities that overwhelm the child's resources. In the pages of ‘The Piggle’, we can see a young child ‘playing’ with the unfathomable notions of beginnings and endings in the wake of the birth of a sibling, which also heralds the death of the extant mother/child relationship. In the case material these issues are explored from the perspective of an adult who had been unable to resolve them without great loss of self. In both cases, parental failures impeded the child's ability to integrate his or her experiences. The work of Matte-Blanco provides a useful terminology for understanding condensations and equivalences in the material; as affect intensifies, the rules of conventional logic recede, and sameness threatens to annihilate distinctions between good and bad, or self and other. Containing these dichotomies within the analytic setting facilitates their integration, in a movement from the fragmentation of the paranoid-schizoid position towards the reconciliation and renunciation of the depressive position.

responseSet.title: Countertransference Past And Present: A Review Of The Concept
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Countertransference Past And Present

A Review Of The Concept

Theodore Jacobs

Theodore Jacobs

170 East 77th St., Apt. 1G
New York
NY 10021

Theodore Jacobs  

My purpose in this paper is to review the major trends and developments in the evolution of the concept of countertransference. I will do so from my own perspective; that is, from the viewpoint of one American analyst, trained in a classical institute, who has had a long-standing interest in this issue.

Although I will attempt to describe some of the questions and controversies that have surrounded the idea of countertransference as well as the viewpoints of many of those who have written on the subject, I will make no attempt to be all-inclusive. My effort, rather, will be to present an overview of a concept, long in the shadows, that has emerged as one of the issues most actively discussed and debated in psychoanalysis today.

Looking back on the final decades of the twentieth century, in fact, future historians of psychoanalysis may well designate this period the countertransference years; for in this time few concepts in our field have gained as much attention, have been as widely explored and written about, and have been the subject of as much controversy as has the question of countertransference and its role in the analytic process. Certainly in America, but also, to a considerable extent worldwide, countertransference and the closely related issues of inter-subjectivity, enactments, self-analysis and the question of neutrality have taken centre stage as matters with which contemporary analysts are much preoccupied.

One could say, then, of countertransference, that it is a concept whose time has come; or perhaps more accurately, that it is a concept that, like the proverbial groundhog, has emerged into the sunlight when the conditions were right after having previously poked its head into the air, tested the weather, and retreated below ground. The creature who has now appeared, however, is essentially the same one who did the initial reconnoitring; more sprightly and energetic, perhaps, but basically unchanged. What has prompted his re-emergence is not some fundamental alteration in himself. It is a change in the climate, a change in the analytic atmosphere that has made it possible for the familiar face of countertransference to reappear.

responseSet.title: Moving Ahead: Integrating Influences of Affective Processes for Development and for Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Moving Ahead

Integrating Influences of Affective Processes for Development and for Psychoanalysis

Robert Emde

Robert Emde

4200 East Ninth Avenue, C268-69
Denver
CO 80262

Robert Emde  

The author argues that psychoanalytic clinical thinking has evolved towards an organisational model of affect and that multidisciplinary research broadens this thinking. Integrative influences of affective processes have been given little attention in psychoanalytic theory. Such influences are reviewed using examples from research in early development. Affective processes are shown to provide integrative influences across systems in an individual's development, facilitating developmental change, as well as developmental continuity. In a related vein, affective processes provide incentives for the development of both individuality and connectedness with others. The notion of an ‘affective core of self is updated and the important psychoanalytic idea of emotion schemas of self in relation to other is reviewed. Integrating influences of affective processes in psychoanalysis operate mainly non-consciously and are in need of further study.

responseSet.title: Tender Love and Transference: Unpublished Letters of C. G. Jung and Sabina Spielrein
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Tender Love and Transference

Unpublished Letters of C. G. Jung and Sabina Spielrein

Zvi Lothane

Zvi Lothane

1435 Lexington Avenue
New York
NY 10128

Zvi Lothane  

The author dissents from the widely accepted interpretation that the relationship between Sabina Spielrein and Carl Jung in the years 1904–1910 included sexual intercourse and constituted an ethical breach of the doctor-patient boundary in the course of a treatment. Spielrein declared that her treatment ended with her discharge from the Burghölzli hospital as Jung's patient in 1904–1905. Jung maintained he ‘prolonged the relationship’ in order to prevent a relapse and also referred to it as a friendship. Materials published in 1994 (letters, drafts, diaries, hospital chart) and unpublished letters recently found by the author in the Claparède archive in Geneva shed new light on previously published documents and interpretations by Carotenuto that have dominated the secondary literature since 1980. The new materials provide a more nuanced view of the Spielrein-Jung relationship and point to the function of non-erotic love in the therapeutic relationship. A new look at the Freud-Jung correspondence about the Spielrein-Jung relationship shows that Jung's perception that a sex scandal was initiated by Spielrein resulted from Jung's misreading of rumours concerning another woman; the episode had no ill effect on the relationship between Freud and Jung.

responseSet.title: The Oedipus Complex Revisited: Oedipus Abandoned, Oedipus Adopted
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Oedipus Complex Revisited

Oedipus Abandoned, Oedipus Adopted

Danielle Quinodoz

Danielle Quinodoz

53A Chemin des Fourches
1223 Cologny (Geneva)

Danielle Quinodoz  

The author considers that the Oedipus of Sophocles' drama, who kills his biological father and marries his biological mother, is an illustration of the failure to work through the complex named after him. She draws attention to the contrast between the obscurity that surrounds the hero's adoptive parents and the notoriety of their biological counterparts, arguing that the former are equally important in the universal oedipal fantasy. In her view the fact that Oedipus has two sets of parents—abandoning and adopting respectively—is significant in that the resulting dichotomisation of the parental imago enables him seemingly to avoid a conflict of ambivalence in relation to a single object. The author further contends that the plague of Thebes represents the symptomatic return of repressed or disavowed aggression. Three clinical examples reveal the parental imago's division to be an unconscious defence against oedipal anxieties and the sense of solitude vis-à-vis the intimacy of the parental couple. The working through of the Oedipus complex is stated to require the synthesis of love and hate and of the abandoning and adoptive aspects of the parents, whereby the formerly sadistic superego becomes protective. The author also argues that a useful distinction can be made between splitting and dichotomisation.

responseSet.title: Memory and Therapeutic Action
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Memory and Therapeutic Action

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Gower Street
London WC1E 6BT
P.Fonagy@UCL.AC.UK

Peter Fonagy  

The aims of psychoanalysis have been greatly elaborated over the hundred years since Freud's original model of undoing repression and recovering memory into consciousness (see for example Sandler & Dreher, 1997). But these advances have not brought with them an updating of the role of memory in the therapeutic process, nor a clear and consistent theory of therapeutic effect. Some still appear to believe that the recovery of memory is part of the therapeutic action of the treatment. There is no evidence for this and in my view to cling to this idea is damaging to the field.

While no one nowadays would explicitly espouse Freud's earliest model (Breuer & Freud, 1895), its legacy is clear in later models of therapeutic change, including Freud's own (1914).1 Greenacre, for example, expresses concern that the repetitive confrontation and interpretation of drive and defence as manifested in current life including the transference could lead to a neglect of the exploration and reconstruction of early memories and an ignoring of the need to ‘work to’ traumatic recollections (1956, pp. 644-5). Notwithstanding Kris's (1956) revolutionary reformulation of the psychoanalytic understanding of memory, the majority of ego-psychologists have continued to place emphasis on the therapeutic efficacy of reconstruction for a variety of clinical (Blum, 1980; Rosen, 1955; Sachs, 1967) and theoretical reasons (Schimek, 1977, 1987). Nor has the modern emphasis on the therapeutic relationship as the primary motor of therapeutic action, for which we are indebted to Winnicott (1956) and Loewald (1960), succeeded in eliminating the emphasis on the recovery of childhood experiences.

It is commonly argued that it is the ‘presence’ of the analyst as he experiences the past that leads to the change of meaning and the reorganisation and re-evaluation of early experience (see, for example, Viederman, 1994). Doubts about the accuracy of early memories have prompted some theoreticians to deemphasise the role of past experience altogether and favour accounts of therapeutic action in terms of the mere integration of past accounts and the achievement of a higher level of narrative coherence (e.g. Schafer, 1976, 1980; Spence, 1984). Some have taken issue with the assumption that the patient's verbal narrative is not a reliable source for promoting shared meaning with the analyst (Sass & Woolfolk, 1988; Wetzler, 1985). Setting aside the issue of accuracy, the fact that analytic material consists primarily of constructions of the past in the present still implies that therapeutic action lies with the analyst addressing the nature of the patient's retrospective disclosures. The extension of the concept of Nachträglichkeit

responseSet.title: Flight Into Sanity: Jones'S Allegation Of Ferenczi'S Mental Deterioration Reconsidered
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Flight Into Sanity

Jones'S Allegation Of Ferenczi'S Mental Deterioration Reconsidered

Carlo Bonomi

Carlo Bonomi

Borgo Pinti 87
I-50121 Florence

Carlo Bonomi  

In ‘The Life and Work of Sigmund Freud’, Volume III, Ernest Jones explained Ferenczi's final contributions as the product of a mental deterioration based on a progressive psychosis. Erich Fromm collected various testimonies by witnesses of Ferenczi's last years, all contrasting with Jones's assertions, and challenged Jones's manner of writing history. However, since Fromm was himself a dissident, and his witnesses were pupils, relatives or friends of Ferenczi's, they were discarded as ‘partisans’. The present study aims at reconsidering the question of Ferenczi's insanity on the basis of many unpublished documents. The consulted documents do not support Jones's allegation of Ferenczi's insanity. At the same time, they show that Jones's allegation was not a one-man fabrication, but reflected a shared belief, eliciting many questions about the nature of this belief, the lack of scrutiny that characterised its spreading, and its possible function within the psychoanalytic community. It is suggested that Ferenczi's personality and teaching, especially his emphasis on the need to accept the patient's criticism, contrasted with the dominant conception of psychoanalysis, based on the analyst's infallibility.

responseSet.title: The Secret: Study Of A Perverse Transference
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Secret

Study Of A Perverse Transference

Louise Carignan

Louise Carignan

326 Somerset West, Suite 200
Ottawa
K2P 0J9

Louise Carignan  

This clinical communication describes the analysis of a perverse transference in an overtly bisexual man. An intricate interplay between heterosexual and homosexual relations enabled him to maintain some heterosexual functioning and to counteract or evacuate intolerable affects and vengeful impulses—originating from early traumas—through perverse acting out with sexual partners. Building on the notion that patients with sexual perversions recreate aspects of their perversion in the transference/countertransference relationship, where it must be analysed, the author illustrates through detailed clinical material how she was drawn into the fabric of her patient's perversion and how this was worked through. She offers an object-relational understanding of his perversion as a complex erotised defensive/reparative/vengeful manoeuvre permitting the disavowal and magical reversal into triumph and sexual pleasure of perceived threats to his personal or sexual identity. Engaging or changing in analysis evoked fears of losing his identity by surrendering to the analyst, rooted in the patient's early experience of serving as his mother's fetish-object. The author suggests that the patient needed to turn the analyst into a perverse partner (disavowing her other-ness) in order to reverse and triumph over this core trauma. She attributes the good treatment outcome to the detection and working through of the perverse transference and monitoring of her countertransference.

responseSet.title: Freud's Early Clinical Theory (1894–1896): Outline and Context
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Early Clinical Theory (1894–1896)

Outline and Context

Ulrike May

Ulrike May

Taunusstr. 12
12161 Berlin

Ulrike May  

The author points out that all Freud's publications and lectures from the period 1894–1896 had the aim of establishing an aetiological theory of the neuroses and psychoses. Freud found that the clinical pictures covered by the theory had a sexual aetiology and were distinguishable from each other by specific mechanisms. Numerous references are presented to show that what the author calls Freud's early clinical theory had its roots in contemporary neuropathology and psychiatry, and the prevailing views in these disciplines are discussed. He was able to build on the work of predecessors such as Möbius in regard to the causal role of psychic factors. Whereas Freud was of his time in attempting to redefine the category of ‘neurasthenia’ and adducing the familiar concept of ‘sexual noxae’, his originality lay in the elevation of these factors, hitherto seen as external, to the status of ‘true’ causes. In the author's view, the rejection met with by Freud's early clinical theory was due to its pre-experiential ‘vision’ of the sexual aetiology of the neuroses rather than to its content. She notes that Freud differed from his contemporaries on the concept of and systematic part played by sexuality, as well as on the central role of psychic mechanisms (i.e. of defence).

responseSet.authorMast: Robert S. Wallerstein and  Peter Fonagy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Research and the IPA

History, Present Status and Future Potential

Robert Wallerstein and Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

(Dr Wallerstein)
655 Redwood Highway, Suite 261
Mill Valley
CA 94941

London

Peter Fonagy  

This paper traces the chequered history of formal psychoanalytic research within the century long development of psychoanalysis as theory and practice. It describes the official involvement of the American Psychoanalytic Association in the support of psychoanalytic research since 1970, and of the International Psychoanalytical Association since the 1980s. In the IPA, this has consisted of the introduction at the 1987 Montreal Congress of two half-day panels on formal psychoanalytic research; in the creation in 1991 of an annual IPA Research Conference held in March at University College London; and then the creation in 1996 of an annual ten-day IPA Summer School for neophyte psychoanalytic researchers, also at UCL in London. The paper then details the inauguration by the IPA, at the 1997 IPA Congress in Barcelona, of a mechanism for funding psychoanalytic research, the Research Advisory Board (RAB), and the overwhelming—and totally unanticipated—worldwide response to the initial call for proposals in the autumn of 1997. Implications of this for psychoanalysis as a discipline are sketched.

responseSet.title: Erotic Complications
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Erotic Complications11

Joyce Slochower

Joyce Slochower

15 West 75th St.
New York
NY 10023

Joyce Slochower  

The author argues that erotic transference—countertransference dynamics present particular complexities when they develop between gender constellations other than male analyst and female patient. She addresses the dynamics of a complicated erotic transference in concert with an aversive countertransference response as it evolved between a female analyst and female patient. The intense erotic transference that developed defied classification as either maternallerotic or oedipallerotic, and instead included both features in a rapidly shifting process that was difficult to address analytically. The analyst's confused, often aversive, response to her patient's erotic wishes ultimately revealed a subtle re-enactment involving split-off and erotised experiences of emotional penetration and scrutiny. When these issues were addressed, the erotic transference dissolved, and the analyst's experience of her patient shifted rather dramatically. It is suggested that complex erotic transference sometimes contains within it evidence of previously repressed object experiences that were not primarily sexual in nature.

responseSet.title: Guest editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Guest editorial

J. Burland

J. Burland

5 Colwyn Lane
Bala-Cynwyd
PA 19004

J. Burland  

Dear Sir,

I am responding to Donald B. Spence's Guest Editorial (79, 4: 643-647), which concerned my paper ‘The role of working through in bringing about psychoanalytic change’ (78, 3: 469-484). First, let me say that it is nice that someone read my paper, gave it careful thought, and chose to write a commentary about it. I am sure that all authors of published articles see the psychoanalytic literature as a debate in which views are exchanged, and that one's article stimulates discussion is one of the rewards of participating in it.

I am familiar with Dr Spence's ideas, and recognize them in his editorial. I share his skepticism about the written case report or clinical case vignette, although I also believe that they are an important and useful tool in our scientific debate, historically and currently, flawed and imperfect as they are. The psychoanalytic encounter is inherently messy and complex. The participants are consciously aware of only a small part of what is transpiring at any moment, and this is multiplied exponentially when trying to conceptualize the totality of a process that has gone on for hundreds of hours over many years. What the participants do perceive they perceive through glasses that, like an amusement park's fun-house mirrors, impose a unique and individual shape upon what is perceived. What is remembered is doubly altered as the process of recall itself adds a second layer of re-editing to what was perceived subjectively in the first place. That, I am sure, is common knowledge to all thoughtful psychoanaysts. Perhaps the temptation to cling to theories and seek reconfirmation of them in the clinical data is a way of dealing with the anxiety caused by this very messiness.

However, the fact is that in the real world, that is how we do what we do, and it seems to me that we cannot let that deter us from trying, as best we can, to step back from our work, examine it, and try to find language and concepts that allow us to think and write about our work and discuss it with colleagues, approximate and inadequate though these efforts may be. I am sure Dr Spence would agree that simply because we are trying to find a way to talk about something that is hard to talk about does not mean we should throw up our hands, throw in the towel, and throw the baby out with the bath water.

responseSet.title: ‘The recovered memories controversy’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘The recovered memories controversy’

Richard Chefetz

Richard Chefetz

2 Wisconsin Circle, Suite 210
Chevy Chase
MD 20815-7003

Richard Chefetz  

I would like to thank Dr Valeria Egidi Morpurgo for her bibliographical help and Amanda Watson for her editing of these notes.

Dear Sir,

The International Journal of Psychoanalysis is to be commended for its attention to issues regarding memory and neuroscience, illustrated by the space allocated to expositions on consciousness and memory in Volume seventy-nine, Part five. However, I was quite dismayed with the ‘Book Review Essay: The Recovered Memory Controversy’ by Mary Target (pp. 1015-1028). While some of her work was intellectually crisp, there were other areas which limped along, and some which were grossly misleading and/or unsupported by the data. This statement is a case in point: ‘On the analyst's side, why would he or she look for, or accept as accurate, the reporting of previously forgotten sexual abuse, given the evidence that such recovered memories are very likely to be mistaken?’ (p. 1025).

375

responseSet.title: The Inner World in the Outer World. Psychoanalytic Perspectives: Edward R. Shapiro. New Haven, Connecticut: Yale University Press. 1997. Pp. 187
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Inner World in the Outer World. Psychoanalytic Perspectives

Edward R. Shapiro. New Haven, Connecticut: Yale University Press. 1997. Pp. 187

Helen Gediman

Helen Gediman

55 East 87th Street, #1B, New York, NY 10128

Helen Gediman  

In ‘Thoughts for the times on war and death’ (1915; S.E. 14), Freud established a tradition that was to acknowledge the reciprocal influences of inner and outer worlds. The outer world, seen at that time primarily through the lens of the atrocities of the First World War, was viewed as mainly evil. Its influences on the inner workings of the mind were limited, by and large, to the commonly held instinctual impulses, neither good nor bad, of all mankind. The inner world accommodated to outer civilisation mainly by instinctual renunciation and transformations. Freud's paradigm is revisited and expanded, implicitly, in this current volume, which emerged from an international conference held at the Austen Riggs Center in celebration of its seventy-fifth anniversary.

Its editor, Edward R. Shapiro, urges nine contributors, all distinguished and eminent psychoanalysts who are known for their work in selected areas of the field, to explore the boundary of experience where inner and outer worlds converge. He suggests as an integrating schema for the contributions to this book that the limited focus by psychoanalysts and all dynamic therapists on the internal world alone may have contributed to the field's inability to stay in touch with the effects of massive social change on the treatment setting. Each of the contributors to this volume is guided by his or her own special interests. Therefore, the variety of perspectives on the shifting boundaries of inner and outer vary considerably in the ways that they respond to Shapiro's suggested integrating schema. Had the authors complied with the letter, the outcome could have been a procrustean and limited discourse. Had all the contributors literally stayed close to their charge, the compendium might not have had the implicit tensions between authors that lends it the excitement it conveys. Fortunately, as individual psychoanalytic thinkers each taking up the particular banner of their choice, they ventured to one degree or another outside the boundary of the suggested task, expressing their inner takes on the topic. The result at worst could have been a melange of individualistic self-expressions conforming more or less to the general topic each was invited to discuss. As it turns out, however, we are treated to a most interesting and multi-perspectival work that consists of highly individualised variations on a unifying theme.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Open Minded: Working Out the Logic of the Soul

Jonathan Lear. Cambridge, MA and London: Harvard University Press. 1998. Pp. 342

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg

122 South Michigan Avenue, Suite 1305b
Chicago, IL 60603

Arnold Goldberg  

An artist friend of mine once suggested to me that I might benefit from trying to look at some of my familiar sights—perhaps on a routine walk—in a different way, say from a changed perspective or by concentrating on a detail that I had not much noticed. I was initially surprised to realise how much of my seeing had become captured by habit, indeed how I seemed only to be alerted to the deviations in scenes that I had become so accustomed to seeing, so that even a slight change from routine became more of an irritant than a curiosity. I have no doubt that this comfort of familiarity is a member of many a psychoanalytic household, and so, many of us prefer even our reading to fit the regularity of life as well as of our ideas about psychoanalysis. Now comes an artist on the scene, one who gently coaxes us to look at things anew.

Actually this artist is not a new arrival to the analytic scene, and it seems he has been in our midst for some time, and so perhaps that is why he stirs curiosity without irritation. However, the first thing that one must say about this latest book of Jonathan Lear's is that it is filled with eye-openers. From the mere adjusting of or squinting our eyes up to the open-mouthed startle of discovery, each of these essays in a book aptly titled ‘Open-Minded’ forces us to see things that we thought were all too familiar as remarkably fresh. Although one can never be sure if the readership for this book is meant to be a newcomer to psychoanalysis or a hardened veteran of the analytic scene, and while some of us may squint and others may stare, I suspect that nothing in psychoanalysis will look quite the same after (as the book's sub-title names it) the workout.

409

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Remembering Trauma: A Psychotherapist's Guide to Memory and Illusion

Phil Mollon. Chichester, England: John Wiley & Sons. 1998. Pp. 221 + xv

C. Brenneis

C. Brenneis

2700 Marshall Court, Madison, WI 53705
brenneis@facstaff.wisc.edu

C. Brenneis  

For most psychoanalysts, the ‘Memory Wars’—that battle over the authenticity of memories of trauma recovered in psychotherapy—may be being fought in a distant land, between parties none of whom raise great sympathy or interest, and about matters that seem relatively far removed from everyday analytic practice. This is, I think, a mistaken perspective; for the Memory Wars, in a very basic sense, engage issues fundamental to analytic practice. At the centre of this debate lie questions about the impact of real past events on psychological development, about the reliability of memory and our capacity to discern past from present, about dissociation as a defence against trauma, and about the vulnerability of the therapeutic process to suggestion. When a seasoned clinician with significant psychoanalytic leanings and a very broad acquaintance with the relevant scientific literature presents a careful look at this mess, it is worth paying attention to. In Remembering Trauma, Mollon accomplishes two ends; he reviews and critically summarises a diverse band of information relevant to the problem of recovered memories; and, based on his conclusions, he offers a series of guidelines for responsible psychoanalytic practice.

Memory and memory for trauma receive considerable attention. Mollon uses an impressively heterogeneous collection of information—clinical cases, clinical, laboratory, and biological research, and emerging theoretical models of memory—to illuminate memory's complexities, especially in response to trauma. Memory, as is now universally accepted, is a reconstructive process, shaped by the present as well as the past, and consequently responsive to a fair amount of internal and external influence. Two systems of memory exist side by side—explicit and implicit. Explicit memory is directly available to consciousness and includes the memories of personal experience that help define our conscious sense of self. Implicit memory registers outside of consciousness and may be demonstrated only in action, as in habitual and skilful behaviours, conditioned responses and perceptual priming.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Getting in on the Act

The Hysterical Solution’ By Ronald Britton

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

15 Arden Road, London N3 3AB
williams@psyc.demon.co.uk

Paul Williams  

Britton's paper appeared on the IJP web-site from 13 January until 19 March, and was published in the journal in 1999, 80: 1-15. The discussion began slowly but gained momentum rapidly, following several contributions on the interesting clinical questions raised. Using the case of Anna O and one of his own, Britton postulates that a feature of hysteria is the use of projective identification by the patient to become in phantasy one or other member of the primal couple. A symbolic version of the primal scene between patient and analyst may then be enacted in the context of the erotic transference–countertransference that characterises these cases. Britton suggests that what we know now about the case of Anna O makes better sense in the light of modern psychoanalysis.

Freud privately divulged undisclosed details of the Breuer–Pappenheim case to Ernest Jones who included them in his biography of Freud (Jones, 1953, pp. 246-8). However, in one crucial detail Jones remembered the story wrongly; namely the timing of Breuer's wife's pregnancy. The conception of the child did not follow the termination of Bertha Pappenheim's treatment, as Jones wrote, but ran in parallel with it (Ellenberger, 1970, p. 264). The child was born on 11 March 1882 when Anna O was still in treatment. Britton suggests that correction of the timing of the pregnancy enables us to make more sense of the case.

In the light of this Britton offers a concise, detailed review of the story of Bertha Pappenheim and her treatment at the hands of Breuer (see paper). He highlights the crucial moment among many when Breuer and his wife went away, during which month their daughter was conceived. Anna O was an intimate of the Breuer circle and would have discovered the existence of the pregnancy. Britton points out that it is in this context that Anna O obliterated the present in her hysterical states and returned in daydream to living in the previous year, behaving as if she were in her old room. In the spring of 1882 when Breuer's new child was in early infancy and presumably at her mother's breast, Anna O developed a new symptom, ‘she suddenly found it impossible to drink’ (Breuer & Freud, 1895, p. 34). It does not seem fanciful to re-interpret this drinking phobia and recounted recollection by Anna O as a reaction to her phantasies of Mrs Breuer giving the new baby a drink from her breast. As an infant of 16 months she probably also witnessed the feeding of her newly born baby brother.

responseSet.title: Deconstructing Dirty Harry: Clint Eastwood's Undoing of the Hollywood Myth of Screen Masculinity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Deconstructing Dirty Harry

Clint Eastwood's Undoing of the Hollywood Myth of Screen Masculinity

Ronald Baker

Ronald Baker

12 Southwood Lawn Road
London N6 5SF
ronald.baker@virgin.net

Ronald Baker  

Play Misty For Me (1971)

Director: Clint Eastwood

Distributor: Universal/Malpaso

Clint Eastwood attained super-star box office status as ‘The Man with No Name’ through Sergio Leone's spaghetti westerns in the sixties and Don Siegel films, especially Dirty Harry (1971), in which he played the title part. Eastwood exemplified the prototypical macho male Hollywood hero: cold, refractory, cruel, sadistic, murderous, ferocious and seemingly invulnerable, his callous contempt and abuse extending no less to women than to men. However, in his directorial debut, Play Misty For Me (1971), Eastwood cast himself as a character whose vulnerability contrasted markedly with his iconic screen persona, a portrayal that anticipated themes that recurred ever more poignantly in his subsequent films.

In Misty, Eastwood plays an all-night disc jockey Dave Garver, a philanderer who has jeopardised his relationship with a girlfriend, Tobie (Donna Mills). They have temporarily parted but it is evident that he wishes to rebuild that relationship. A girl regularly requests Erroll Garner's ballad Misty, which he occasionally plays. One night Dave is picked up by Evelyn (Jessica Walter), the girl who likes Misty, in a bar. They agree to a one-night-stand with ‘no strings’. Evelyn turns out to be a morbidly jealous and murderous psychotic. She invades Dave's life with ever-increasing intimidation and violence, including verbal abuse, stalking and humiliation in public. Later she slashes her wrists, tears his house apart, attacks him with a knife, severely injures his maid and murders a policeman investigating the case. When Evelyn is caught and imprisoned, Dave and Tobie negotiate a brief reconciliation. However, Evelyn is unexpectedly released and the terror resumes. She moves in with Tobie (who had not met her previously) as a room-mate, ties her up, threatens to murder her and stabs Dave when he tries to rescue her. A struggle ensues, Dave punches her over a balcony and she falls to her death. In the final scene Dave, bleeding and shaken, leans dependently on Tobie as she helps him to the car.

responseSet.title: Freud's Models of the Mind: An Introduction: Joseph Sandler, Alex Holder, Christopher Dare and A. Ursula Dreher. London: Karnac Books. 1997. Pp. 203
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Models of the Mind: An Introduction

Joseph Sandler, Alex Holder, Christopher Dare and A. Ursula Dreher. London: Karnac Books. 1997. Pp. 203

Elizabeth Spillius

Elizabeth Spillius

20 Achilles Road, London NW6 1EA

Elizabeth Spillius  

This excellent book is a testimony to the fact that teaching is father to learning, for the chapters of which it is composed began life, as the authors say in their Introduction, as a series of lectures at the Institute of Psychiatry at the Maudsley Hospital. The twelve lectures were published in the British Journal of Medical Psychology between 1972 and 1982. Various dog-eared offprints have found their way around the world, especially to the United Kingdom, the United States and Israel where teachers and students of Freudian psychology have found them to be an enlightening map of Freud's developing thought. So it is a great pleasure, nearly thirty years after the publication of the first lecture, to have all the lectures united into a book at last, added to and amended a bit, but retaining their original freshness and usefulness. The book is a reminder of the clarity of Sandler's teaching and it increases once again our regret and sorrow at his untimely death.

Sandler and his co-authors divide Freud's thinking into three main frames of reference: the affect-trauma model from the mid-1880s to 1897, the topographical model from 1897 to 1923, and the structural model from 1923 to 1939. This is an admirable delineation, though perhaps a little confusing when one is talking to Continental or Latin American colleagues, for they customarily speak of only two models, the topographical and the structural, evidently regarding the affecttrauma model as an early phase of the topographical model.

After the Introduction the book begins with a brief description of the three frames of reference, followed by a statement of basic psychoanalytic tenets. The authors make it clear that they strive to deal only with the essential outlines of Freud's thought, omitting debates about the finer points that have exercised Freud scholars for many years. Nor do they debate the validity of Freud's basic assumptions about drives (‘instincts’), the unconscious, the ‘mental apparatus’, determinism and adaptation. They make it clear too that there is no single ‘right’ psychoanalytic theory, Freud's or any other. Freud did not revise his theories so thoroughly that his later ones entirely superseded the earlier ones, nor have later theories entirely superseded Freud's.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Severe Sexual Inhibition in the Course of the Psychoanalytic Treatment of a Patient with a Narcissistic Personality Disorder11

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg

Westchester Division
21 Bloomingdale Road
White Plains, NY 10605

Otto Kernberg  

This case report illustrates how an analysis of oedipal conflicts gradually resolved a severe and extended inhibition of sexual desire that developed as a new symptom in the termination phase of psychoanalytic treatment. The enactment in the countertransference of castration anxiety, against which the patient was successfully defending himself by projective identification, produced an extended stalemate, which was resolved once the counter-transference was transformed into transference interpretations. This treatment also illustrates the intimate connection between pre-oedipal and oedipal conflicts in the advanced stages of the treatment of narcissistic personalities, and the need for very careful assessment of the patient's sexual functioning before deciding on terminating the psychoanalysis of a patient with a successfully resolved narcissistic personality structure.

responseSet.title: Diversity and Direction in Psychoanalytic Technique: Fred Pine. New Haven, CT: Yale University Press. 1998. Pp. 218
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Diversity and Direction in Psychoanalytic Technique

Fred Pine. New Haven, CT: Yale University Press. 1998. Pp. 218

Salman Akhtar

Salman Akhtar

1201 Chestnut Street, Suite 1503
Philadelphia, PA 19107

Salman Akhtar  

Fred Pine's book is an eloquent plea for the integration of diverse aspects of psychoanalytic theory and technique. It is handsomely produced and fascinatingly written. I will say more about Pine's writing style after addressing his book's content. This essentially centres upon the relevance of and need for heuristic and technical pluralism in psychoanalysis. Far from wishy-washy eclecticism and firmly rooted in a developmental perspective, Pine's book offers a carefully structured argument for such pluralism. He lays out the evidence bit by bit and at each step tackles potential counterpoints, allows caveats and permits the reader a glimpse of his own evolution as a theoretician and practising analyst. Pine regards psychoanalysis as an expanding field in which the theoretical and technical changes are additive and not substitutive. For him, ‘the psychoanalytic, the psychotherapeutic, and the developmental are closely blended’ (p. 3). He states:

I often think of my clinical work in terms of doing as much psychoanalysis as possible in context where I do as much psychotherapy as necessary—the latter being precisely what makes it possible to pursue the former. At other times, or with other patients, the psychotherapeutic portions seem a full partner of equal status and value in the enterprise. I do not quite think of these latter treatments as psychoanalysis, but I can not think of them as not psychoanalysis, since psychoanalysis inevitably enters into the work so significantly for an analyst (p. 3).

Such ‘disciplined flexibility’ (p. 14) characterises the development of ideas throughout the book. Regarding technique, for instance, Pine is firmly committed to the ‘trio of guideposts’ (p. 13) of neutrality, abstinence, and anonymity and yet comfortable with the idea that ‘there has to be room for personal judgment and for subtle but significant variations in the realities of individual clinical situations’ (p. 14). Indeed, Pine regards psychoanalytic technique as ‘inherently variation prone’ (p. 15) and takes on the difficult task of discerning what such variations are, how they arise in the clinical situation, what dictates their necessity and what their attendant risks are.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Otto Fenichel: 119 Rundbriefe (1934–1945), Bd. I, Bd. II. [Otto Fenichel: 119 circular letters (1934–1945)], Vols 1 and 2

J. Reichmayr and E. Mühlleitner Reviewed by Elisabeth Brainin

Elisabeth Brainin

Elisabeth Brainin

Halbgasse 6/31, 1070 Vienna

Elisabeth Brainin  

Not long ago Otto Fenichel's circular letters were published in an edition distinguished by excellent editing, with a clearly structured index and also a CD-ROM containing the entire text. The onerous task assumed by the two editors and the risk taken on by the small publishing house have borne fruit for every psychoanalyst. These two volumes are of historical and scientific interest to the psychoanalytic community, bringing alive as they do the development of our science, its various trends and the differences within the psychoanalytic movement, and placing them in their historical context.

The circular letters are a document of the times that reflects the main currents within psychoanalysis with all the clarity that was Fenichel's forte. Otto Fenichel was a psychoanalyst from Vienna who worked at the Berlin Institute until 1933. There he belonged to a group of young left-wing psychoanalysts known as the ‘children's seminar’, to whom he subsequently addressed his circular letters. They included a smaller circle, comprising Georg Gerö, Samuel Golan, Edith Gyömröi-Glück, Edith Jacobson, Barbara Lantos, Käthe Misch-Friedländer, Annie Reich and, until 1934, Willi Reich and Nic Hoel. Alice Balint, Therese Benedek, Berta Bornstein, Erich Fromm, Angel Garma, Martin Grotjahn, Abraham Kardiner, Sándor Radó, René Spitz, Heinrich Winnik and others were members of the wider group of circular letter addressees. All these names are familiar to every psychoanalyst today. Fenichel himself embarked on an odyssey after the Nazi seizure of power in Germany: moving to Oslo and then to Prague, he finally landed in the United States. One of the central figures of psychoanalysis, he was responsible for a ‘theory of the neuroses’ that remains in the curriculum of most psychoanalytic training institutes today. His enormous energy enabled him to tackle a workload sufficient for a whole group of people. He engaged in clinical treatment, teaching and theoretical work, all of which he pursued indefatigably with the aim of the further development and application of Freud's positions. Reichmayr and Mühlleitner call him the historiographer of the psychoanalytic movement, and this is documented by the ‘circular letters’. They were directed to this small, originally Berlin-based, circle of analysts who were particularly concerned to apply psychoanalysis to social processes.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

L'adolescent dans la psychanalyse—l'aventure de la subjectivation [The adolescent in psychoanalysis: the adventure of becoming-a-subject]

Raymond Cahn. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. Collection Le Fil Rouge. Pp. 202

Françoise Bouchard

Françoise Bouchard

19 avenue Théophile Gautier, 75016 Paris

Françoise Bouchard  

Raymond Cahn's new book follows the paper ‘Du sujet’ [The subject] (1990) presented to the Congress of French-Language Psychoanalysts and his theoretico-clinical work Adolescence et folie—les déliaisons dangereuses (1991).2

The volume under review is divided into three long chapters: (I) the adolescent in psychoanalysis (psychopathology and metapsychology); (II) the adolescent and psychoanalytic treatment; and (III) adolescence, culture and society.

It is worth preceding our discussion of the book as a whole with a note on the author's definitions of adolescence and his conception of the process of becoming-a-subject.3 These vibrant notions recur frequently during the course of the book. In Cahn's view, ‘adolescence is the period when the coming together of biological, psychic and social factors completes the long path of development of the young of the human species from neonate to adulthood’ (p. 7). ‘However, the subject of psychoanalysis does not appear on the stage fully equipped with its definitive psychic apparatus.’ The subject is in fact the culmination of a long process, adolescence being the vital phase in which new modalities of psychic work are created. It is the critical period of every possibility. Here, ‘becoming-a-subject’ is defined as ‘a process that gradually establishes the distinct existence of subject and object, the necessary distance between self and self’ (p. 185). The author notes elsewhere that there can be ‘no subject without an object’, but that the interrelationship between the two is conditional upon disengagement and dealienation from the power of the other—from the object that is, all the same, essential to the subject's psychic life. Subjective appropriation begins at birth and continues until the ‘concluding phase’ that permits—or, as the case may be, does not permit—the establishment of a personal psychic space and ‘the possibility of an internal process of transformation and creation’ (p. 2). Cahn sees in these capacities ‘the condition for the possible unfolding of an analytic process in later life’—an aspect examined in the subsequent chapter on the indications for analysis at this stage of life. Adolescence is the time when ‘the vicissitudes of the relationship between the return of the same and the emergence of the new prove decisive’. In telling us that becoming-a-subject is a process of differentiation rather than of separation-individuation, Cahn invites us to acknowledge the importance of the integration of the subject/object concept, the significance of the distinction between word presentations and thing presentations, the difference between internal and external reality, the vital role of self-to-self capacity and the difference between thought and action; all these processes of differentiation are structured out of the depressive position, castration anxiety and the oedipal conflict. In this period of transformation and transition, the adolescent is confronted with a personal renewal that may constitute either a structural crisis or a destructive destablisation—albeit with a potential ‘astonishing capacity for recovery’.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Understanding Trauma: A Psychoanalytical Approach

Caroline Garland. London: Duckworth. 1998. Pp. 212

Jill Scharff

Jill Scharff

6612 Kennedy Drive, Chevy Chase, MD 20815

Jill Scharff  

Understanding Trauma is a collection of high-quality chapters that apply analytic principles to the understanding of trauma, each written by an analyst or therapist from the Tavistock Clinic Adult Department's Unit for the Study of Trauma and its Aftermath. The authors' work with trauma ranges from individuals to community groups and covers the traumas of train crash, hotel fire, crushing by a soccer crowd, blinding and disablement from a burning car, sudden attack and beating by a violent patient, rape, sibling suicide, near-fatal medical error and random murder. Having collaborated to study trauma, the authors have developed a shared orientation, which gives the book a coherence that is lacking in many edited books. At the same time, each chapter stands alone, which means that many of them repeat basic concepts.

The book takes as its starting-point Freud's ideas on the stimulus barrier, mourning and melancholia, the compulsion to repeat and punishment from a sense of unconscious guilt. Also prominent is Freud's concept of binding of excitation, used in different ways at different places in the book. As part of her masterful review of classical theory at various stages of Freud's development, Garland introduces binding as a defensive process that is intensified by trauma. She holds that this leads both to fusion and to keeping open pockets of vulnerability. I thought that she might have linked this idea to the encapsulation of trauma that she later describes as a foreign body in the mind and the result of a failure of symbolic thinking. In a later chapter, David Bell invokes the concept of symbolisation as representation to link Freud's concept of binding anxiety with Klein's use of binding as a process of managing anxieties of annihilation and fragmentation so that they can be modified.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Internal Landscapes and Foreign Bodies. Eating Disorders and Other Pathologies

Gianna Williams. London: Duckworth. Tavistock Clinic Series. 1997. Pp. 149

Jane Temperley

Jane Temperley

89 Canfield Gardens, London NW6 3EA

Jane Temperley  

This book is one of a series designed to present aspects of work at the Tavistock Clinic. Gianna Williams is a Consultant Child and Adolescent Psychotherapist drawing on extensive experience in the Adolescent Department and especially in its workshop on eating disorders. She writes with a lucidity that makes her subtle psychoanalytic understanding accessible to the general public as well as illuminating to her colleagues. There is an abundance of case illustrations demonstrating how the child's internal world comes to be constituted as it is and how this can be understood and eventually modified in the child's interaction with the therapist.

Most of the clinical illustrations are of intensive long-term work with very traumatised and deprived adolescents, but Williams illustrates the same processes in detailed observation of mothers and infants and some painful studies of young children in crèches and institutions.

These patients have not been able to establish in their internal worlds ‘an object who loves and protects the self and is loved and protected by the self’ (pp. 123-4). In some of Williams's cases, spoiling and destructive forces within the personality, that begrudge acknowledging a helpful object, prevent the internalisation of such a figure. She traces psychoanalytic awareness of these forces back to the Rat Man and comments on how Freud's exoneration of that patient from moral responsibility for the violence of his unconscious was superseded in Mourning and Melancholia by a recognition of how such unconscious attacks affect our internal objects and our self-esteem.

The main emphasis in this book is, however, not on spoiling and perverse impulses but on environmental factors that have prevented the child from establishing an object capable of what Bion described as containment, the ability to process emotional tumult and make some sense of it: Williams contrasts how we are born equipped with digestive systems in our bodies with the way in which, on the other hand, we need the experience of a parental mind capable of bearing and processing our raw emotional states before we can establish within ourselves the capacity to think, to digest mentally. The patients with whom she worked had histories of gross deprivation, long periods of institutional care and multiple separations. Lacking parental containment, their equipment for processing emotional intake was defective and that emotional intake was itself intolerable. They are handicapped by a double deprivation.

responseSet.title: Ermeneutica e narrazione. Un percorso tra psichiatria e psicoanalisi [Hermeneutics and narration: a journey between psychiatry and psychoanalysis]: Giuseppe Martini. Turin: Bollati Boringhieri. 1998. Pp. 337
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ermeneutica e narrazione. Un percorso tra psichiatria e psicoanalisi [Hermeneutics and narration: a journey between psychiatry and psychoanalysis]

Giuseppe Martini. Turin: Bollati Boringhieri. 1998. Pp. 337

Maria Ponsi

Maria Ponsi

Via della Chiesa 77, 50125 Florence

Maria Ponsi  

The hermeneutic tradition and narratology—two fields of knowledge repeatedly visited by both psychoanalysts and psychopathologists—are assigned a central position in this volume, which, according to the author himself, constitutes ‘an attempt to supply new theoretical foundations for psychiatric activity’ by informing it with psychoanalysis (p. 3). By keeping the dialogue between the two disciplines open, the author seeks to integrate them on the level of both theory and clinical practice.

The book is divided into two parts, ‘Method’ and ‘Practice’. The first begins with an excursus on narratology and hermeneutic philosophy, with the aim of facilitating a conceptualisation of psychiatry and psychoanalysis as a narrative exchange and as the search for and construction of meaning.

The author identifies three possible channels of access for psychoanalysis to hermeneutics: (1) the non-representational dimension of the unconscious as manifested in psychotic situations; (2) clinical approaches directed towards the here and now of the transference, where the analytic enterprise centres on the construction of a shared reality; and (3) the interpersonal and intersubjective aspect of the analytic dialogue.

In discussing the positions of the many authors who have grappled with the hermeneutic perspective (such as Schafer, Spence, Hoffman and Hanly in North America, and Chianese, Corrao, Laplanche, R. Steiner and Strenger in Europe)

responseSet.title: The Impact Of A War Experience On The Inner World Of A Young Child
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Impact Of A War Experience On The Inner World Of A Young Child

Silvia Goldie

Silvia Goldie

40 Parkhill Road
London NW3 2YP

Silvia Goldie  

The author uses material from the treatment of a young child whose development became seriously arrested from the time of a traumatic experience during a war. This experience, combined with the impact of the birth of a younger sibling while he was still a baby himself, had catastrophic consequences. He could only communicate his feelings of terror and fear of dying through the use of projective identification. The persecutory anxiety of this child was such that for a while he could not tolerate hearing the interpretations, and was prone to violent outbursts. The author describes how she had to bear the projections and manage the physical attacks while trying to maintain the capacity to observe and think clearly. This setting produced an experience of containment that gradually allowed this child to accept and understand interpretations which diminished the power of feelings that had overwhelmed him in the past. Through introjection of the experience of being understood during his sessions, he began to develop the capacity to think about his feelings. This allowed him to gradually recover, develop, and make use of his intelligence and imagination, and the behavioural difficulties that brought him to treatment disappeared.
