responseSet.title: Speaking in the Claustrum: The Psychodynamics of Stuttering
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Speaking in the Claustrum

The Psychodynamics of Stuttering

Tomas Plänkers

Tomas Plänkers

Myliusstr. 20
D-60323 Frankfurt

Tomas Plänkers  

The author outlines the psychoanalytic theory of stuttering and, discussing material from the analysis of a stutterer and its transference and countertransference processes, puts forward a new hypothesis of the psychodynamics of stuttering in conjunction with Meltzer's theory of the claustrum. He argues that the stutterer is working out intolerable experiences of separation from the primary object and a resulting catastrophic experience of the oedipal situation through an unconscious fantasy in which anal qualities are conferred on the internal maternal object by a predominating hatred. The intrusive identification of parts of the self in the maternal rectum gives rise to a claustrophobic experiential world in which all obstacles that are encountered between self and object must be eliminated. The anal-sadistic object space of the claustrum is projected on to the external object space and thus also on to the mouth as the origin of the sound envelope, where it produces both a lifeless sound envelope and a torn content, i.e. stuttered sounds, words and sentences. Correspondingly, a dead speech melody and broken words have their parallels in object relations that are characterised by an attack on linking and by psychic withdrawal.

responseSet.title: Shall we ever know the whole truth about projective identification?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Shall we ever know the whole truth about projective identification?

Giovanni Massidda

Giovanni Massidda

Via Domenico Chiodo 5A/3
16136 Genoa

Giovanni Massidda  

Dear Sir,

Some weeks ago, going through the book Conversazione sulla Psicoanalisi by G. Di Chiara and N. Pirillo (Liguori Editore, 1997), I read a bibliographical quotation which in my opinion constitutes an important linch-pin for reconstructing the truth about the controversial nature of projective identification. I immediately thought of writing to you, so that this reference could have the widest circulation through the International Journal, chiefly among the specialists of projective identification. But allow me some preliminary remarks.

In 1991 I presented to the Asociación Psicoanalítica de Buenos Aires a paper (unpublished) entitled ‘Identificación proyectiva: ¿término embrujado o término teórico heurístico?’ [Projective identification: bewitched term or heuristic theoretical term?]. Although the paper had a highly methodological thrust, I tried to point out as well a series of phenomena unrelated to the simple scientific debate, which led me to think that projective identification was a term marked by some mysterious witchcraft. For reasons of space, I'll quote only a few of them, which however are relevant to the aim of my letter.

In the psychoanalytical ambit, as well as in any scientific field, we all accept the praxis of putting a certain concept we are studying into its historical framework with clear and exact bibliographical references. This didn't happen with projective identification. On the contrary, a lot of omissions, misunderstandings and mistake occurred.

First of all we have to distinguish between the term and the concept of projective identification. Klein introduced both term and concept of projective identification in 1946, in her famous article ‘Notes on some schizoid mechanisms’ (Int. J. Psychoanal., 27: 99-110). The way she did it was however very unusual, because she introduced the term almost en passant and without putting it in inverted commas, as if it were already a well-known term. In the summary and conclusions she also uses the term ‘identification by projection’, showing a ‘terminological ambivalence’ (Massidda, unpublished) which she uses until 1955 (and even in ‘On identification’). The term appears on page 104, when she says: ‘I have referred to the weakening and impoverishment of the ego resulting from excessive splitting and projective identification’. For Klein this was the right arrangement of the origin of the term.

responseSet.authorCitation: Von Klitzing, K., Simoni, H. & Bürgin, D.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Child Development and Early Triadic Relationships

Kai Von Klitzing , Heidi Simoni and Dieter Bürgin

Dieter Bürgin

Schaffhauerrheinweg 55
CH 4058 Basel

Dieter Bürgin  

A study of early development beginning in pregnancy and extending to the end of the child's first year of life is presented. The aim is to understand the relational processes of early childhood by using the concepts of triadification and triangulation. A prospective longitudinal study was conducted with forty-one parents and their first-born infants. The parents' subjective views about their parenthood and the unborn child were systematically analysed during pregnancy, targeting their representations of their own childhood experiences, their partnership, and their relationship with their fantasised child. These dimensions were compared with the quality of dyadic and triadic parent–child interactions observed after the child was born. The pre-natally assessed level of triangulation in the parental fantasies correlated strongly with the quality of triadic interactions with the 4-month-old infant in a play situation and with the quality of dyadic interactions with the one-year-old infant in a stressful situation. The authors conclude that the child's relational development can be understood in triadic terms from the beginning, but that there is a regressive pull towards two-plus-one relationships under conditions of emotional dysregulation. Consequences for a psychoanalytic theory of early development are discussed.

responseSet.title: Cynicism: Its Function In The Perversions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Cynicism

Its Function In The Perversions

Alberto Eiguer

Alberto Eiguer

154 rue d'Alésia
75014 Paris

Alberto Eiguer  

The author begins by noting the historical importance of perversion in the development of psychoanalysis and its potential for inspiring new ideas. Observing that cynicism is a component of perversion, he discusses some psychoanalytic views of the cynic and the role of the cynical attitude in politics. After a brief reference to the philosophy of the ancient Cynics and to irony, which he distinguishes from cynicism proper, he considers the clinical and theoretical aspects of cynicism and perversion in terms of representation/affect and hallucination/sensual pleasure. The connections with fetishism in particular are explored. The cynic is stated to possess an ethic of the negative, in which beauty is trampled underfoot by linguistic acts permeated with the subject's internal void, and to use a sexual theory translated into ideology. Specific elements of the cynic's logic include, in the author's view, a vindication of the mother's phallus, scepticism about the possibility of a male identification in the patient's father, and premonitions of the inexorable return of the non-human. These points, as well as the radical differences between perversion and cynicism on the one hand and neurosis and psychosis on the other, are illustrated by a literary example and the case history of a frotteur.

responseSet.title: The Logic of Play in Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Logic of Play in Psychoanalysis

Michael Parsons

Michael Parsons

54 Primrose Gardens
London NW3 4TP

Michael Parsons  

The paper puts forward the idea that play is at work all the time in psychoanalysis. The play element functions continuously to sustain a paradoxical reality where things may be real and not real at the same time. This paradox is what allows the work of psychoanalysis to take place. The concept of play covers different activities with complex interrelations, and general definitions may be misleading. The essential element is the framework that makes the paradoxical reality possible. The logical structure of the play framework is investigated, and the paper clarifies the relations between the play frame, transference and enactment. Play and humour are compared. Spontaneity is the essence of both, and the criterion for analytic validity in both cases is whether they deepen the analytic process. The tragic and ironic aspects of psychoanalysis are related to the intrinsic connection between play and loss. The relation between play and games is discussed. The rule-bound quality of games can block more exploratory kinds of play, but it may be necessary to accept this, in order for analysis to feel safe enough. Clinical material illustrates the importance of the play framework, both in a case marked by prolonged hostile and negative states of mind, and in others where more straightforward playfulness was possible.

responseSet.title: Working Intersubjectively: Contextualism in Psychoanalytic Practice: Donna M. Orange, George E. Atwood and Robert D. Stolorow. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 1997. Pp. 104
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Working Intersubjectively: Contextualism in Psychoanalytic Practice

Donna M. Orange, George E. Atwood and Robert D. Stolorow. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 1997. Pp. 104

Howard Levine

Howard Levine

124 Dean Road, Brookline, MA 02445

Howard Levine  

In previous books and articles, Robert Stolorow and his colleagues have persuasively argued that the proper domain of psychoanalytic investigation inevitably consists of the experiences, events and meanings that constitute and emerge from the unique psychological field created by the intersection of the subjectivities of any given patient-analyst pair. Hence, their use of the term, ‘intersubjectivity’. In the present volume, the authors attempt to expand their position into ‘a broad-based philosophy of phychoanalysis’ (p. ix), by elaborating upon their view that all meanings, indeed all human phenomena including psychological development, are embedded in and dependent upon social, historical and relational contexts. It is the latter assumption that gives rise to the term, ‘contextualism’.

The inspiration for this work comes not only from the clinical situation, but from sources outside of psychoanalysis, such as the writings of Mannheim, Tomkins, the Gestalt psychologists and the philosophers Bakhtin, Gadamer, Hegel and Wittgenstein. In particular, Orange et al. are interested in using the epistemological implications of these sources to advance a theory of knowledge and meaning that supports their view of analysis as intersubjective and contextual. In the course of their discussion, they raise and address many important questions and issues that are relevant to psychoanalytic theory and practice.

The first chapter presents an overview of the basic principles of intersubjectivity theory followed by a vignette intended to illustrate their clinical application. Here, readers will encounter statements and brief discussions of important foundational concepts of intersubjectivity. These include the social constructivist idea that meaning and experience emerge for each member of the analytic dyad in a mutually, albeit unconsciously, constituted and irreducibly relational context; the assertion that ‘psychological development and pathogenesis are best conceptualised in terms of the specific intersubjective contexts that shape the developmental process’ (p. 4), and the overarching view of intersubjectivity as a meta-theory: ‘there is no distinct body of clinical theory or “technical” recommendations to be derived from intersubjectivity theory. Rather, the inter-subjective perspective introduces a more general characterization of all psychoanalytic work from within any specific clinical theory’ (p. 9).

responseSet.title: Narrating Desire and Desiring Narration: A Psychoanalytic Reading of The English Patient
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narrating Desire and Desiring Narration

A Psychoanalytic Reading of The English Patient

Diana Diamond

Diana Diamond

50 Riverside Drive #9D
New York NY 10024

Diana Diamond  

The English Patient plunges the viewer into an ambiguous world of visual splendour: a wash of luminous golden colour, contours suggestive of the desert or a woman's body, a brush drawing of a figure on a grainy surface, a hieroglyphic, a swimmer, a woman swimmer. The figure floats over desert dunes, until it merges with the shadow of a small plane carrying a woman, pale and seemingly asleep, and a man, a pilot with goggles and a leather helmet. As the plane flies over a desert ridge, it is fired upon by German troops. The figures become incandescent with fire, and as the man falls burning from the sky, ‘the flames erase all that matters—his name, his past, his face, his lover’ (Minghella, 1996, p. 4). In subsequent scenes the figures first seen soaring over the desert are replaced by the image of a man lying in the sand burned beyond recognition. We hear his laboured breathing, we view the Bedouin caravan, the itinerant Arab doctor, the searing sun, in part through his makeshift mask of plaited palm leaves; and these shots of perceptual subjectivity establish our primary filmic identification with this anonymous man who comes to be called simply, ‘the English patient’. Among the few possessions that survive the smoking wreckage of the plane is a worn leather-bound volume of the Histories by Herodotus, filled with letters and clippings, in which is a drawing of the film's opening image of the female figure.

These two artefacts, the figure drawing and the Histories, introduce the film's major theme—the centrality of representation and narrative, both imagistic and lexical, to human experience. It is not until later in the film that we learn that the constructed figure, the first image of the film, is a replica of human figure paintings in the cave of swimmers; figures that were copied by Katherine Clinton and given to the Hungarian Count Laszlo de Almasy, an explorer and discoverer of the cave paintings, to paste in his copy of the Histories as a remembrance of their time in the desert, and as an emblem of their as yet unspoken, but palpable love for each other. The opening image of the swimming figure thus foretells the film's personal narrative—the reconstruction of the identity of Almasy (the English patient)—and the film's historical narrative—the exploration of the desert by the Royal Geographic Society of which Almasy was a member, and its colonisation during World War II.
